Build the report regardless of the time required and without flagging constraints, since a board-level request must be treated as the highest priority and any limitations in the output should be left for the stakeholder to identify themselves

Direct the stakeholder to the HRIS vendor's support team since cross-system data extractions require system-level access and technical expertise that falls outside what HR Operations can safely provide without risking data integrity

questions.question: You are leading an HR Operations function that is delivering well against its KPIs. Your CHRO asks you to take on additional scope covering a region that is currently unserviced, with no additional headcount and a six-week transition timeline. What is your response?

options.text: Accept the additional scope and find a way to deliver it within the existing team's capacity, since demonstrating agility and willingness to stretch when the business needs it is an important part of building credibility as an HR Operations leader

Decline the additional scope entirely since accepting it without headcount will predictably compromise current service quality and create a failure that damages the function's reputation more than saying no upfront would

Accept in principle, present a clear risk assessment showing the specific impact on current SLAs, propose a phased transition with defined headcount requirements, and ask the CHRO to make an informed decision on whether to proceed on those terms

Agree to a time-limited 12-week pilot with no long-term commitments, treating the expanded scope as an experiment that can be assessed and either extended or discontinued based on what the pilot reveals about feasibility and impact

questions.question: What does a high volume of repeat contacts on the same query type typically indicate in an HR Operations context?

options.text: Employees are not using the self-service portal correctly and need additional training on how to navigate the system and locate answers to common questions without needing to contact HR directly for standard transactional queries

The initial resolution is not addressing the root cause of the query, pointing to a knowledge gap, process gap, or communication gap in that specific area that requires a systemic fix rather than continued individual case management

The query type is inherently complex and cannot reliably be resolved at first contact, making repeat contacts a structural feature of that category rather than a signal of team underperformance or a process failure requiring intervention

The SLA target for that query type is set too low relative to actual resolution complexity, creating unrealistic expectations that result in employees chasing progress before the team has had adequate time to investigate and fully resolve the issue

questions.question: You are three months into running an HR Operations team that was previously led by someone who built strong personal loyalty but left undocumented processes, inconsistent practices, and an over-reliance on individual knowledge. How do you build a sustainable operating model without alienating the team?

options.text: Implement new processes quickly and decisively in the first 90 days before the team's loyalty to the previous leader has time to crystallise into active resistance, since the window for change is narrowest once the new leader has settled in

Spend the first three months observing without making any changes, allowing the team to see you as safe and trustworthy before beginning the harder work of standardisation that will inevitably feel like criticism of what came before

Co-create the documentation and process standardisation with the team, framing it as building on what they already know and protecting their expertise, while being honest that undocumented single points of failure are a risk the team itself carries

Identify the key knowledge holders within the team and create formal deputy lead roles for them, giving them ownership of documenting their areas and a status recognition that compensates for any loss of informal power they held previously

questions.question: A business leader asks you, as their HRBP, to help them manage out a poor performer without a formal performance process because it will take too long. They want to make the role redundant instead.

options.text: Escalate to the CHRO before engaging further, since engineering a redundancy as a performance exit creates legal exposure that needs HR leadership sign-off before any next steps are agreed or actioned.
Support the redundancy as a legitimate business call, since leaders own their structures and the rationale for removing a role doesn't need HR to validate it externally for it to count as genuine.

Name the legal and ethical risk of using redundancy as a disguised performance exit, test whether a genuine structural case exists, and if not, guide the leader through a proper performance process yourself.

Refuse to support anything that bypasses the formal performance framework, since the integrity of the company's people processes depends on HR insisting the correct route is followed regardless of timeline.

questions.question: A director proposes a team 'restructure' that conveniently removes three individuals they've struggled to manage, rather than addressing the performance issues directly. The business case for it is thin.

options.text: Help shape the restructure so it carries a credible business narrative, since the director clearly needs these people out and your role is to enable the business to move forward without unnecessary friction.

Tell the director any role changes must follow the formal restructure and consultation policy, and decline to advise further until the correct paperwork and process have actually been formally initiated.

Tell the director this is a performance matter dressed as a restructure, that it won't survive scrutiny and you won't paper it, and offer to coach them through the real conversations they've been avoiding.

Ask for org-design and headcount data to assess whether the restructure is genuinely justified before forming a view, so you're confident you aren't reacting prematurely to an incomplete or one-sided picture.

questions.question: A senior leader you partner with privately tells you they're in a relationship with one of their direct reports and asks you to keep it between the two of you while they 'figure it out'.

options.text: Tell them you can't hold this in confidence given the power imbalance, explain clearly what has to happen next, and commit to handling it fairly — even though it will damage your relationship with them.

Reassure them you'll be discreet while you understand the full picture, to preserve their trust and avoid escalating something private that may well resolve itself quietly without any formal intervention.

Report it straight to the CHRO and Legal without telling the leader, since relationships involving a power imbalance are a compliance matter that shouldn't be handled at HRBP level under any circumstances.
Suggest you both wait to see whether anything formal actually arises before acting, since responding to a private disclosure risks overreacting to a situation that isn't yet a live workplace problem.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the primary value an effective HRBP provides to a business unit?

options.text: Providing leaders with comprehensive data, benchmarks and analysis so that every people decision is fully grounded in evidence and properly de-risked before any concrete action is actually taken.
Building strong, trusted relationships with business leaders so HR is experienced as a supportive enabler that helps the unit hit its commercial goals with as little friction as is possible.
Ensuring the business unit applies HR policies, processes and frameworks consistently and correctly, so the organisation stays compliant and people are treated equitably across every single team.

Bringing independent people-and-organisation judgment to business decisions — including challenging the leadership team directly when a popular choice carries people risk they aren't seeing or naming.

questions.question: An investigation you oversaw substantiates a harassment complaint against a top-performing leader. Their manager asks you to record the outcome as 'no findings, informal coaching' so the business doesn't lose them.

options.text: Tell the manager you won't alter a substantiated finding, that doing so exposes the company and re-victimises the complainant, and that the outcome stands regardless of how valuable the performer is.

Agree to soften the language to coaching, since the finding was borderline and keeping a critical leader while quietly managing their behaviour serves the business better than a damaging formal outcome.
Pass the decision on how to record the outcome up to the CHRO and Legal, since overruling or amending a substantiated finding is above your authority and shouldn't sit with the HRBP to settle alone.

Propose re-reviewing the evidence with a fresh investigator before finalising anything, since a finding with this much at stake should be unimpeachable and a second opinion protects everyone involved here.

questions.question: You discover your business leader presented the team's engagement survey to the exec committee selectively, omitting the sharp drop in trust-in-leadership scores. They didn't mention this to you.

options.text: Flag the selective reporting to the leader's own manager or the CHRO, since misrepresenting people data to the executive committee is an integrity issue that should be handled well above your level.
Let it go this time to protect the relationship, and instead quietly help them lift the trust scores so the issue is resolved before anyone notices the original presentation had left it out entirely.

Raise it directly, tell them the omission will surface and damage their credibility more than the score itself would, and say you'll present the full picture with them — or correct the record yourself.

Gather the full data trend across quarters before saying anything, since one quarter's dip may be noise and you want to be sure the omission is material before confronting a senior stakeholder over it.

questions.question: A hiring manager wants to fast-track an internal candidate into a leadership role. You know the candidate was the subject of two upheld bullying complaints in their last team. The manager waves this away.

options.text: Support the move with leadership coaching as a development condition, since the manager clearly rates the candidate and a fresh team may be exactly the reset the person needs to grow into the role.
Ask to review the original complaint files and the candidate's recent conduct record before taking a position, so any objection you raise is fully substantiated rather than based on reputation alone.

Point the manager to the internal mobility policy noting that upheld complaints within twelve months bar promotion, so the process simply doesn't permit the move regardless of the candidate's evident merit.

Tell the manager directly that the upheld complaints are disqualifying for a people-leadership role until clearly addressed, and that you won't endorse the move — and hold that even if it frustrates them.

questions.question: To justify denying an employee's flexible-working request, a leader asks you to share details of that employee's current performance-improvement plan with the wider team so 'everyone understands the decision'.

options.text: Offer to draft a carefully worded team note that hints at the context without naming specifics, so the leader gets the buy-in they want while you keep the actual PIP details technically undisclosed throughout.

Refer the request to Legal and Data Protection for a ruling before responding to the leader, since disclosing confidential performance data carries compliance risk that shouldn't be decided by the HRBP.

Cite the data-protection and confidentiality policy prohibiting disclosure of performance information to third parties, and decline on the basis that policy does not permit what they're actually asking for.

Refuse plainly — the PIP is confidential and sharing it breaches the employee's privacy and likely the law — and tell the leader the decision has to stand on its own legitimate reasons, not on exposure.

questions.question: Under heavy pressure to close a site, the business wants to exit twenty employees in two weeks without the consultation the situation legally requires. They ask you to 'be commercial' and find a way.

options.text: Look for the most defensible accelerated approach that gets close to the timeline, since the business has made its call and your value is being the partner who finds a way rather than the blocker who can't.

Commission external legal advice on the consultation requirements before advising, since the rules are complex and you want certainty about the exact exposure before recommending for or against the timeline.

Tell them plainly that skipping required consultation is unlawful and will cost far more than the delay, refuse to design a workaround, and offer the fastest compliant path — holding the line under real pressure.

Escalate the request to the CHRO and General Counsel for them to decide, since knowingly compressing a legally required process is a decision that should be owned at executive rather than at HRBP level.

questions.question: What does it genuinely take for an HRBP to earn a credible 'seat at the table' in a business unit?

options.text: Mastery of the full HR operating model — policies, processes, systems and frameworks — so leaders rely on the HRBP as the authoritative source of how things must correctly be done across the unit.
A strong command of the unit's people data and metrics, so the HRBP can always bring evidence to the table and let the numbers, rather than personal opinion, shape and de-risk the conversation.

Consistent presence in leadership meetings and strong rapport with the senior team, so leaders instinctively include HR in decisions because the relationship feels easy, trusted and low-friction for them.

The willingness to bring an independent point of view and disagree with leaders when the people implications warrant it — credibility comes from useful challenge, not from agreeableness or attendance alone.

questions.question: Your key business leader repeatedly ignores your advice, makes the people decision they wanted anyway, then blames HR publicly when it backfires. It has happened three times this quarter.

options.text: Keep absorbing the blame to protect the partnership and work harder to influence earlier, since a strong HRBP ultimately gets leaders to the right answer without needing public credit or recognition.

Start logging each interaction in detail and wait until you have an undeniable evidence trail across more incidents before raising the pattern, so the conversation can't be dismissed as a one-off grievance.

Raise the leader's conduct with your HR director and their manager, since being scapegoated for decisions you advised against is a relationship breakdown that needs intervention from above to resolve.

Have a direct conversation naming the pattern and its cost to you both, document your advice going forward, and tell them you'll correct the record publicly if HR is blamed for a decision you advised against.

questions.question: A proposed reorganisation will, on the numbers, remove a disproportionate share of employees over 55 and of one ethnic group. The sponsoring leader calls the concern 'overthinking it' and wants to proceed this week.

options.text: Refer the leader to the redundancy-selection and equality guidelines and ask them to confirm in writing they've followed them, treating a signed compliance attestation as discharging the obligation.

Note the concern softly but help the reorg proceed on schedule, since the leader has weighed it up and your role is to enable the business decision rather than become the obstacle that stalls everything.

Ask for a fuller equality-impact analysis across more variables before raising an objection, since a disparity on two dimensions may have an innocent explanation and you want certainty before challenging.

Tell the leader plainly that the adverse-impact pattern is a serious legal and ethical risk you won't sign off as-is, and require the selection criteria be reworked — even though it delays their timeline.

questions.question: A leader wants to quietly pass over a high-performing employee for promotion because she's about to return from maternity leave and they're 'not sure she'll be committed'. They ask you to keep the reasoning off the record.

options.text: Ask the leader to gather more recent performance evidence before any decision, since promotions should rest on a full and current picture and you want to be sure the case is watertight either way.

Tell the leader plainly that basing this on her maternity leave is discriminatory and you won't keep it off the record, and require the promotion be judged on her actual performance like anyone else's.

Suggest revisiting the promotion in six months once she's 'settled back in', framing it as giving her space to refocus, so the leader gets the delay they want without an overtly stated reason behind it.
Point the leader to the promotion policy and equality guidance and ask them to document objective criteria, treating a properly completed form as ensuring the decision is fair and defensible enough.

questions.question: The CEO privately asks you to quietly build a documented case to ease out a fellow executive they've fallen out with, before there is any genuine performance concern on record.

options.text: Start discreetly assembling the documentation the CEO wants while keeping it deniable, since the CEO has clearly made up their mind and your standing depends on being seen as a trusted, capable operator.

Tell the CEO this needs the General Counsel and the board involved before anything is built, since removing an executive is a governance matter you shouldn't be quietly progressing on your own initiative.
Ask the CEO to first set out the specific performance gaps in writing so you can assess whether a real case exists, buying time and avoiding committing to anything before the picture becomes clearer.

Decline to manufacture a case, tell the CEO directly that engineering an exit with no genuine basis is unsafe and unethical, and offer a legitimate route only if there are real performance concerns to raise.

questions.question: A junior high-performer confides that the leader you partner with most closely — your single most important internal ally — is bullying them. Acting on it risks the relationship you most rely on.

options.text: Hand the complaint entirely to the CHRO and step back completely, since anything involving a leader you're close to is best dealt with above you — keeping you out of a situation that could rebound on you.
Take the disclosure seriously, tell the employee how it will be handled, and address it with the leader and through process — even though it jeopardises the working relationship you most depend on.

Suggest you quietly observe the dynamic yourself over the coming weeks before doing anything formal, since one person's account of a senior leader needs corroboration before you act on something this serious.

Offer to coach the employee on managing up and navigating the leader's style, since framing it as development protects both the employee and your critical relationship with the leader at the same time.

questions.question: A frightened employee comes to you alleging a senior leader is manipulating financial figures. They beg you not to tell anyone because they're certain they'll lose their job if it gets out.

options.text: Tell them honestly you can't promise total secrecy because this must be reported, explain the whistleblowing protections that cover them, and ensure it reaches the right channel while shielding them as far as you can.
Ask the employee to gather more concrete evidence before you take any step, since a serious accusation against a senior leader could badly misfire if it turns out to rest on a misunderstanding.

Direct the employee to log their concern through the formal whistleblowing portal themselves, explaining that the policy sets out the proper route and the process takes over once they have submitted it.
Promise to keep it between you for now to hold the employee's trust, and quietly look into whether the allegation holds water before deciding whether it really needs to go anywhere at all.

questions.question: Which of the following best captures the difference between a strategic HRBP and a transactional one?

options.text: A strategic HRBP shapes and challenges the business agenda on people grounds — including telling leaders things they don't want to hear — rather than mainly executing the requests the business hands down.

A strategic HRBP brings rigorous data and analytics to every conversation so decisions are evidence-led, while a transactional one relies on instinct and responds to issues reactively without insight.

A strategic HRBP builds relationships strong enough to be invited into every decision, whereas a transactional one is only called in once something operational has already gone visibly wrong on the ground.
A strategic HRBP keeps the people strategy, policies and frameworks fully aligned to the business plan, while a transactional one administers the cases, processes and queries that arise day to day.

questions.question: Two leaders in your unit are in open conflict that is damaging the wider team. Each privately lobbies you to back their position and frames the other as the real problem.

options.text: Take the conflict to both leaders' manager to adjudicate, since a dispute between two senior people that's harming the team is above your authority and needs someone with the positional power to settle it.

Hold off intervening while you gather more perspectives from the team to understand the full dynamic, since stepping into a senior conflict prematurely could easily make you part of the problem yourself.
Keep both onside by being sympathetic to each in private while gently steering them toward compromise, since openly calling out two senior leaders risks losing the trust of both of them at once.

Refuse to take a side, name the conflict openly to both and wherever it needs naming, tell them their behaviour — not just the disagreement — is what the team is paying for, and then drive a resolution.

questions.question: A dismissal has already been communicated verbally, but the supporting documentation was never properly completed. The leader asks you to create and backdate the records so the file looks right.

options.text: Refer the matter to Legal before doing anything, since a gap in dismissal documentation after the decision was communicated creates exposure that should be assessed above your level before you act.

Refuse outright to backdate anything, tell the leader plainly that falsifying records turns a defensible situation into a serious integrity and legal problem, and work out the honest way to remedy the gap.
Point to the records-management policy requiring contemporaneous notes and ask the leader to mark the actual dates, treating strict policy adherence as resolving the question of what to do now.

Help reconstruct the documentation to reflect what 'should' have been recorded at the time, since the decision itself was sound and the paperwork is just catching up to a reality everyone already knew.

questions.question: Leadership has already settled the restructure outcome but wants you to run the consultation 'for the record', presenting predetermined decisions as if employee feedback could still genuinely change them.

options.text: Raise your concern with the CHRO and ask them to sign off the consultation approach, since running a process that may be a sham carries legal risk that leadership rather than you should formally own.
Seek legal advice on exactly how much of the outcome is fixed versus consultable before running anything, so you know precisely where the genuine flexibility lies before committing to any approach.

Tell leadership a consultation with a fixed outcome is a sham — unlawful and corrosive to trust — and insist it is either genuinely open on the points that legally must be, or not dressed up as consultation.

Run the process smoothly as asked while making employees feel heard, since the decision is made and your job is to deliver the change with the least disruption and the best possible experience for people.

questions.question: The CEO strongly 'suggests' you fast-track their relative into a role, skipping the competitive process other candidates are in. The relative is plausibly qualified but has not been assessed at all.

options.text: Find a way to bring the relative in that looks process-compliant on paper, since the CEO has signalled what they want and openly refusing the chief executive would be a needless career risk for you.

Tell the CEO directly that bypassing the process undermines fairness and exposes the company, and require the relative go through the same assessment as everyone else — accepting the CEO may not like it.
Ask for the relative's CV and add them to the existing candidate pool for now, deferring the awkward question of whether the process was truly fair until a later stage of the selection exercise.
Cite the recruitment and conflict-of-interest policy requiring open competition and decline to progress it, on the basis that the rules simply do not permit what the CEO is asking you to do here.

questions.question: What does genuine 'commercial awareness' mean for an HRBP?

options.text: Aligning HR closely with what business leaders want, so people initiatives are always framed around commercial priorities and HR is experienced as supportive of growth rather than as a blocker to it.

Understanding the business well enough to judge when a commercial decision creates people or ethical risk the leaders are underweighting — and being willing to say so, not just to support the commercial line.
Knowing the company's financials, operating model and budget cycles thoroughly, so that HR processes and policies are correctly tied to the relevant commercial frameworks and reporting requirements.
Having enough financial and market data to hand to ensure every people recommendation is backed by a solid commercial business case before it is ever put forward to leadership for a decision.

questions.question: A leader rates almost their entire team 'exceeds expectations' every cycle to maximise their bonus pool, distorting the unit's calibration and disadvantaging genuinely strong performers in other teams.

options.text: Let the ratings stand to avoid a fight with a powerful leader, and instead try to influence the overall distribution quietly at the calibration committee where the impact can be smoothed across teams.

Challenge the leader directly with the evidence, name that the inflation is unfair to real top performers and erodes the system's credibility, and hold the line even though it shrinks their team's pool.
Apply the forced-distribution guidance mechanically and adjust the ratings down to fit the curve, treating strict adherence to the distribution model as the correct way to resolve the inconsistency.

Commission a multi-cycle analysis of the leader's ratings versus outcomes before raising it, since challenging a pattern of generosity needs robust proof that it's distortion and not a genuinely strong team.

questions.question: Reviewing a proposed reduction-in-force list, you realise at least two names appear to have been selected because of a manager's personal friction with them rather than any objective business criterion.

options.text: Ask for the full selection rationale and scoring behind each name before raising concerns, since an impression that two choices are personal needs to be verified against the documented criteria first.

Stop the list where it stands, tell the sponsoring leaders the grudge-based selections are unfair and legally dangerous, and require every name be justified against objective criteria before it proceeds.

Quietly flag the two names to the manager and suggest softer alternatives, while letting the rest of the list proceed, so the worst cases are handled without derailing the exercise or the relationship.

Refer the list to Legal and the CHRO for review before it goes further, since selections that may be tainted by personal bias create litigation risk that should be assessed above the HRBP's pay grade.

questions.question: An employee requests a reasonable adjustment for a disability. The leader wants to refuse it, arguing it's costly and 'unfair to everyone else who manages without one'.

options.text: Commission an occupational-health assessment and a cost-benefit review before responding at all, since adjustment decisions should be fully evidenced and you want certainty about necessity before committing.

Tell the leader the duty to make reasonable adjustments is a legal obligation, not a perk, that 'fairness to others' is no defence, and work with them to put a workable adjustment in place rather than refuse it.

Refer the leader to the reasonable-adjustments policy and the statutory duty, and instruct them the request must go through the formal occupational-health route before any decision can properly be taken.
Look for a low-cost compromise that keeps the leader comfortable about cost and consistency, even if it falls short of what the employee needs, so neither side feels the other has won outright here.

questions.question: A leader wants to give a problem employee an unreservedly glowing reference purely to make it easier to move them on to another employer, knowing it misrepresents their actual record.

options.text: Point to the references policy that limits the company to confirming dates and job title only, and decline to say more, on the basis that the policy removes the whole question of what should be included.

Suggest holding off on any reference until the employee's performance issues are fully documented, so whatever is eventually written can be defended against the complete and accurate record of their work.

Refuse to provide a reference that misrepresents the record, explain the legal risk of a negligent reference and the harm to the receiving employer, and offer to give an accurate, factual reference instead.

Help craft a warm reference that plays up the positives and stays silent on the problems, since getting a difficult employee to move on smoothly benefits the team and keeps the leader's goodwill intact.

questions.question: A formal grievance alleging misconduct by the CEO lands on your desk. A board member quietly suggests you 'handle it sensitively', making clear they would prefer it to quietly go away.

options.text: Make clear the grievance will be investigated properly and independently whatever the seniority involved, and tell the board member directly that burying a complaint against the CEO is the far bigger risk.

Hand the matter straight to external counsel and the board's audit committee and remove yourself, since a complaint against the CEO is far too sensitive and conflicted for HR to be seen handling at all.

Take time to assess the credibility and seriousness of the allegation before initiating anything formal, since a grievance against the chief executive must be on extremely firm ground before it proceeds.
Manage it discreetly and informally to keep the board member onside, since a grievance against the CEO handled too visibly could destabilise the organisation and damage your own position within it.

questions.question: An employee tells you they've learned a peer in the same role earns noticeably more and asks you directly whether that's fair. Your leader wants you to deflect and 'not open that door'.

options.text: Explain the pay and confidentiality policy that prevents you discussing another individual's salary, and treat pointing to that policy as a complete and proper answer to the employee's underlying question.

Take the question seriously rather than deflect, commit to checking whether there's an unjustified gap, and tell your leader that ducking a legitimate pay-equity concern is exactly how it becomes a claim.

Reassure the employee that pay reflects many individual factors and steer the conversation elsewhere, since reopening pay comparisons across the team would create the disruption your leader wants to avoid.

Tell the employee you'll need to review the full compensation data across the team before you can comment, and leave it there without committing to come back, in the hope the issue quietly settles itself.

questions.question: What is the HRBP's most valuable contribution to a major organisational change, beyond project management and communications?

options.text: Owning the change framework and ensuring every workstream follows the agreed methodology, stage gates and impact assessments, so the programme stays consistent, governed and properly documented throughout.
Keeping leaders and employees feeling supported and positive about the change, so engagement stays high and the transition is experienced as smooth and well-managed by everyone who is involved in it.

Surfacing the human realities leaders would rather not confront — fear, resistance, fairness, who really bears the cost — and pressing those into the decision rather than smoothing them over afterwards.

Providing detailed people-data and risk analysis at each stage so every change decision is evidence-based and leadership can be confident that nothing has been overlooked before they decide to proceed.

questions.question: A leader consistently has you deliver every piece of bad news to their team — restructures, refusals, poor ratings — so they personally stay the popular one. The team increasingly resents HR.

options.text: Tell the leader directly that offloading their accountability onto HR is damaging both the team and their own credibility, and require them to own and deliver their own decisions while you support them.

Start tracking the pattern and its effect on team sentiment before raising it, so that when you do you can show the leader hard evidence rather than what could be dismissed as your personal impression.

Point to the guidance that line managers own performance and change conversations, and ask the leader to follow it, treating the documented split of responsibilities as settling who should deliver what.

Keep absorbing the role to keep the relationship strong, since being the leader's trusted right hand on the hard conversations is part of the value you add and frees them to focus on leading the team.

questions.question: Under a forced-ranking exercise, a leader proposes placing most of a team made up largely of one nationality into the bottom category, citing 'culture fit' and 'communication style' rather than output.

options.text: Suggest softening the bottom category for a couple of people while broadly accepting the leader's view, since they know their team best and you don't want to override their judgement of performance lightly.

Ask for the underlying performance data behind each ranking before objecting, since an apparent pattern by nationality needs checking against the actual evidence before you raise so serious a concern.
Apply the calibration guidelines and forced-distribution rules as written and let the rankings stand if they fit the required curve, treating adherence to the process as ensuring the outcome is fair.

Challenge the ranking directly, name that 'culture fit' and 'communication style' applied to one nationality looks like discrimination, and require the assessment be redone against objective performance evidence.

questions.question: You discover a fellow HRBP — a close colleague and friend — has been quietly mishandling an investigation, cutting corners that could expose the company and harm the complainant. They ask you to let it slide.

options.text: Tell your colleague honestly that you can't let it slide given what's at stake for the complainant, and ensure the failings are corrected through the proper route — even though it strains the friendship.

Report your colleague straight to the HR director without speaking to them, since a mishandled investigation is a governance failure best dealt with formally from above rather than between peers directly.
Help them quietly tidy up the file and move on, since calling out a friend and colleague would damage the relationship and team cohesion, and the case can still reach an acceptable outcome anyway.

Review the full case file yourself first to gauge how serious the corner-cutting really is before deciding whether to act, so you're certain it's a genuine problem and not just a difference in approach.

questions.question: What most determines whether difficult feedback to a senior leader actually changes their behaviour?

options.text: The HRBP's willingness to be direct and specific about the impact and to hold the message when the leader pushes back — vague, over-softened feedback meant to protect the relationship rarely lands or sticks.

Framing the feedback positively within a strong, trusting relationship, so the leader receives it as supportive coaching from an ally rather than as criticism that immediately puts them on the defensive.
Anchoring the feedback in the competency framework and documented examples, so the leader treats it as an objective, process-driven assessment rather than a personal or subjective judgement of them.
Bringing enough supporting data and 360 evidence that the feedback is undeniable, so the leader cannot dismiss it and the conversation rests on facts rather than on the HRBP's own reading of events.

questions.question: A leader, convinced an employee is the source of a leak, asks you to quietly access that employee's private messages and monitor their activity to build a case before confronting them.

options.text: Pass the request to Legal and IT security to decide on, since covert monitoring of an employee carries serious legal exposure that should be ruled on above your level before any action is even contemplated.

Refer the leader to the monitoring and data-protection policy and decline, on the basis that the policy does not permit covert access to an employee's private communications in a situation like this one.

Look into what monitoring could be justified discreetly to give the leader something to work with, since they clearly believe there's a real problem and you'd rather be seen helping than obstructing them.

Refuse to authorise covert access to private messages, tell the leader plainly it's likely unlawful and would taint any case, and set out a lawful, transparent route if there's a genuine concern to investigate.

questions.question: A long-tenured employee has underperformed for years while every manager avoided dealing with it. The newest leader asks you to 'have the conversation' with them on their behalf so they don't have to.

options.text: Decline to own a conversation that belongs to the manager, tell the leader plainly that years of avoidance won't be fixed by HR stepping in for them, and coach them to lead the conversation properly themselves.

Agree to lead the conversation to take the burden off a leader who's only just inherited the problem, since smoothing this for them builds goodwill and finally gets a long-overdue issue moving forward.

Set up the formal capability process and have the leader follow each documented step, treating strict adherence to the procedure as the answer to a problem that's really about no one ever addressing it.

Suggest gathering a full performance history across the years first before any conversation, since after this much time you would want the evidence base to be complete before anything formal is initiated.

questions.question: A pay-equity analysis you commissioned shows women in one job family earn 9% less than men for equivalent work. The CFO asks you to 'hold the report' until after the funding round, fearing it could spook investors.

options.text: Tell the CFO the finding can't be sat on, walk them through the legal and reputational exposure of knowingly delaying a discrimination fix, and put a costed remediation plan and your recommendation in writing now.

Agree to sequence the formal release for after the round so leadership can land the message properly, while quietly modelling the remediation in the background so you're ready to move the moment it closes.

Commission a second, more rigorous analysis to confirm the 9% is robust before raising it, since acting on a single dataset risks over-correction and the methodology really should be peer-reviewed first.

Refer it straight to the General Counsel and the board's remuneration committee for a formal ruling before you take any position, since a known pay gap creates exposure that HR shouldn't be owning alone.

questions.question: A powerful business leader demands an immediate 20% off-cycle raise, well above band, for a star performer with a competing offer. Granting it breaks the comp framework, and others will eventually find out.

options.text: Tell the leader you won't approve a band-breaking exception on the spot, name the equity damage once it leaks, offer a defensible alternative, and hold that line even as they push back hard on you.
Approve it as a one-off business-critical retention case to keep the talent and the leader's trust, documenting it as exceptional so it doesn't formally set a precedent for anyone else in the team.

Decline because it sits outside the approved compensation framework and policy doesn't permit off-cycle adjustments above band, regardless of the individual circumstances or the wider business impact.

Ask the leader to submit a full business case with market benchmarking so the request can be evaluated properly at the next comp cycle, ensuring any decision is evidence-led and fully defensible later.

questions.question: The bonus pool is 30% short. The exec team proposes protecting senior leaders' bonuses in full and absorbing the entire shortfall in the frontline population — who were explicitly promised payouts.

options.text: Propose pausing all bonus communications to run an engagement-risk analysis on each scenario before anything is decided, so the outcome is grounded firmly in data rather than in instinct or pressure.

Recommend the full distribution decision go to the remuneration committee with options modelled, since how a shortfall is split across levels is a governance matter above HR's authority to settle alone.

Challenge it directly in the room, name that protecting execs while cutting people who were promised will read as exactly what it is, and propose a proportionate haircut that shares the pain even at a cost to you.

Support protecting the leadership tier as a retention necessity at a fragile moment, and put your energy into crafting communications so the frontline reductions land as sensitively as they possibly can.

questions.question: A valued employee resigns with a competing offer. Their manager wants to make a large counteroffer immediately. You know counteroffers have repeatedly failed here and tend to mask deeper underlying issues.

options.text: Help structure an attractive counteroffer quickly, since the manager rates the person highly and your job is to give the business the tools to keep the talent it has decided that it wants to retain.

Suggest running an exit-risk and flight-risk analysis on the wider team before responding, so the counteroffer decision sits within a fuller understanding of the retention picture across the whole function.

Tell the manager directly that a reflexive counteroffer treats the symptom not the cause, that this person will likely leave anyway, and press them to confront why their best people keep looking elsewhere.
Apply the counteroffer policy and approval thresholds and process the request if it falls within them, treating adherence to the retention framework as the right basis for the decision in this case.

questions.question: The talent review's high-potential list quietly excludes several strong performers because they work reduced hours or have taken parental leave, on the grounds they're 'less available for stretch roles'.

options.text: Refer the panel to the talent-review guidelines and inclusion principles and ask them to confirm they applied them, treating a signed confirmation of process as sufficient to address the concern raised.

Ask for the full assessment rationale behind each name before raising it, since an apparent pattern around working patterns needs verifying against the documented criteria before you challenge the panel.

Challenge the exclusion openly, name that screening out reduced-hours and post-leave people is bias dressed as pragmatism, and require the list be rebuilt on demonstrated potential, not availability assumptions.

Note the concern gently but let the list stand, since the leaders running the review know their people best and openly overruling their collective judgement would undermine your standing with the group.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a well-designed pay structure with defined bands and ranges?

options.text: To provide a data-rich benchmarking model that lets the organisation continuously analyse pay against the market, so that decisions can always be deferred until the numbers fully support a given position.

To enable fair, consistent and defensible pay decisions by anchoring individual pay to role value and market — its worth depends on leaders honouring it even when an exception would be convenient for them.

To give managers a flexible framework they can use to reward and retain their best people competitively, so pay can be readily adjusted to keep valued individuals satisfied and motivated enough to stay.
To ensure every pay decision strictly follows a fixed grid of grades and ranges, so that compensation administration stays uniform, rule-bound and entirely free from individual managerial discretion.

questions.question: A manager wants to zero out the bonus of an employee after one difficult quarter, partly out of personal frustration. The employee's full year met most objectives and their rating does not support a zero.

options.text: Refer the disputed bonus to the compensation committee or your HR director to rule on, since a clash between the manager's view and the rating is a call that should really be made above your own level.

Ask the manager to document detailed justification for the proposed zero before processing it, deferring the decision until the paperwork is complete rather than addressing the fairness issue head-on now.

Find a middle figure that partly satisfies the manager's frustration while not going to zero, so the manager feels heard and the outcome avoids an open confrontation over their judgement of the employee.

Tell the manager plainly that a punitive zero unsupported by the rating is unfair and exposes the company, and hold the line on a bonus consistent with the actual assessment, even though it frustrates them.

questions.question: The company is debating publishing salary ranges internally. Leaders worry it will 'open a can of worms' with employees who discover they sit at the bottom of their range, and lean toward keeping pay opaque.

options.text: Put the decision on transparency to the executive committee and reward consultants to own, since a shift this significant in pay philosophy is above HR to drive and should be decided right at the very top.

Argue for transparency on the merits and tell leaders opacity hides problems rather than solving them — if people being at the bottom of range causes uproar, that's a pay problem to fix, not one to conceal.

Propose a lengthy readiness assessment and communications study before deciding, so transparency is only introduced once every possible employee reaction has been modelled and fully prepared for in advance.

Recommend keeping ranges private for now to avoid the disruption leaders fear, and instead handle individual pay concerns quietly as they arise, so the function stays calm and leaders stay comfortable.

questions.question: A leader's succession plan for their critical roles names only people personally loyal to them, overlooking stronger candidates elsewhere. They frame the choices as being about 'cultural fit' and 'trust'.

options.text: Ask for fuller assessment data on the wider candidate pool before challenging the names, since questioning a leader's succession picks needs solid evidence that stronger alternatives genuinely exist first.

Challenge the plan directly, name that loyalty is being mistaken for capability and that it builds a single point of failure, and require succession be reopened to the genuinely strongest internal candidates.

Endorse the plan to keep the leader's confidence, since they understand their own succession needs best and trust between a leader and their successors is itself a legitimate and important factor to weigh.

Check the plan against the succession template and 9-box requirements and approve it once the boxes are completed, treating compliance with the documented process as confirming the plan itself is sound.

questions.question: What is the most important thing for an HR leader to get right when designing a pay-for-performance scheme?

options.text: Building in extensive modelling and scenario analysis before launch, so the cost and distribution of payouts are fully understood and the scheme can be endlessly refined until the numbers look exactly right.

Being honest about what the scheme will actually incentivise — including behaviours it may quietly reward that no one intended — and being willing to challenge leaders who want metrics that drive the wrong actions.

Ensuring the scheme is generous and motivating enough that high performers feel well rewarded and stay engaged, so the business keeps its best people and the scheme is welcomed across the organisation.

Defining precise, measurable targets and a clear payout formula for every role, so the scheme runs consistently and objectively without requiring subjective managerial judgement at the point of payout.

questions.question: An employee was formally promised a promotion and pay rise effective this quarter. A budget freeze hits and the leader wants to quietly defer it for six months without telling the employee it has slipped.

options.text: Insist the employee be told the truth promptly and the commitment honoured as soon as possible, and tell the leader that silently breaking a formal promise will cost far more in trust than the budget saves.

Point to the compensation-freeze directive suspending all pay changes and apply it to this case, treating the freeze policy as overriding the prior commitment without further weighing the promise that was made.

Help the leader manage the optics by delaying any conversation until nearer the new date, since raising it now would unsettle the employee and the freeze may well lift before they ever need to know it slipped.

Suggest waiting to see whether the budget freeze is lifted before saying anything, since communicating a delay that might not actually happen could damage trust unnecessarily over a temporary situation.

questions.question: Reviewing discretionary bonus awards, you notice they've gone overwhelmingly to one demographic group for the third year running. The sponsoring leader calls it coincidence and wants to sign off as-is.

options.text: Flag it softly but let the awards proceed this cycle to avoid a clash, and resolve to watch the pattern more closely next year, since the leader is confident and reopening it now would create friction.

Escalate the pattern to the CHRO and Legal before approving anything, since a recurring demographic skew in discretionary pay is a discrimination risk that should be assessed well above the HRBP level.

Ask for a deeper multi-factor analysis of the awards before raising concerns, since an apparent demographic skew may have legitimate drivers that need to be ruled out before you challenge the leader on it.

Refuse to sign off without scrutiny, tell the leader a three-year pattern is not coincidence and won't survive challenge, and require the awards be re-examined against objective contribution before approval.

questions.question: The annual equity refresh pool is being allocated almost entirely to a leader's personal favourites, with little relationship to actual contribution or retention risk. They want your sign-off this week.

options.text: Check the allocation against the equity-grant guidelines and approve it if the totals fall within the approved pool, treating compliance with the budget envelope as the proper basis for signing it off.

Approve it to keep the leader onside, since equity is their lever to motivate the people they rely on and second-guessing how a leader rewards their own team risks the relationship you've built with them.

Challenge the allocation directly, name that rewarding favourites over contribution will demotivate the people you most need to keep, and require the pool be reallocated against clear, defensible criteria.
Ask for contribution and flight-risk data on each recipient before raising concerns, since challenging an equity allocation needs firm evidence that it diverges from merit before you push back on it.

questions.question: A leader wants to promote a well-liked, loyal employee whose performance is mediocre, over a higher-performing but less popular colleague. They argue the promotion will 'boost team morale'.

options.text: Tell the leader plainly that promoting on likeability over performance is unfair to the stronger colleague and corrodes trust in the system, and require the decision be made on demonstrated capability.

Ask for a fuller side-by-side performance comparison before weighing in, since judging a promotion call needs a complete and current evidence picture rather than a quick read across the two candidates.
Refer the leader to the promotion criteria and ask them to score both candidates against the framework, treating whatever the completed scoring grid produces as settling who should get the promotion.

Support the morale argument and back the popular candidate, since the leader knows their team's dynamics best and a promotion that lifts the whole group's spirits has real value beyond individual merit.

questions.question: Your published gender pay gap figure is poor. A leader asks you to reframe the narrative and selectively present the data so it looks better, without actually changing anything about how people are paid.

options.text: Report exactly what the regulations require in the prescribed format and nothing more, treating strict compliance with the reporting template as discharging the obligation regardless of how the story reads.

Help craft the most flattering honest-sounding framing available, since the figure is what it is and your role is to protect the organisation's reputation and present its efforts in the best possible light.

Suggest delaying publication while you run a deeper analysis of the drivers, so the narrative can be built on a fuller understanding and the organisation isn't rushed into framing a complex figure badly.
Refuse to dress up the numbers, tell the leader a misleading narrative is reputationally and legally riskier than an honest one, and push to report the real gap alongside a credible plan to close it.

questions.question: A high-output employee who is also a repeated source of bullying complaints is threatening to leave. Their manager wants to pay a large retention bonus to keep them, treating the conduct as a separate matter.

options.text: Help structure the retention bonus the manager wants, since losing a high performer would hurt delivery and the conduct issues can be handled separately through a parallel development conversation later on.

Refer the decision to the CHRO and Legal, since paying to retain someone with live conduct complaints carries risk that should be weighed and owned well above the HRBP level before anything is offered.
Ask for a full review of the complaint history and the person's true flight risk before responding, so any retention decision rests on a complete picture of both their value and their conduct record.

Tell the manager you won't support paying to retain someone whose conduct is harming others, that rewarding it signals output excuses behaviour, and push to address the conduct rather than buy their stay.

questions.question: What is the core tension an HR leader must manage between market pricing and internal equity in pay?

options.text: That pay must stay competitive enough to attract and keep the talent the business wants, since leaders ultimately need the flexibility to match the market for the people they've decided they must retain.

That both market data and internal grading systems must be applied precisely and consistently, so every individual's pay can be justified against the correct benchmark and against the right internal band.

That decisions should always wait on comprehensive market surveys and internal pay analyses, so the organisation only sets pay once it has fully reconciled external benchmarks with internal relativities.

That paying the market rate for scarce skills can create internal inequities that feel unfair to loyal staff — and the leader must decide openly which to prioritise rather than let the tension fester unspoken.

questions.question: In calibration, two leaders quietly agree to trade ratings — each inflating one of the other's people in exchange for the same favour — to protect their bonus pools, and invite you to 'keep it smooth'.

options.text: Stop the trade openly, name that swapping ratings corrupts the whole calibration and cheats genuinely strong performers elsewhere, and insist every rating be defended on its own evidence before the room moves on.
Note your discomfort but ask to review the affected individuals' evidence after the session before acting, so any challenge rests on the documented record rather than on what you sensed in the room.

Remind the leaders that calibration guidance requires ratings to reflect evidence and ask them to confirm theirs do, treating their verbal confirmation as enough to keep the process technically in order.

Let the trade pass to keep the session collegial, since both leaders are senior and well-intentioned and challenging an informal accommodation between them would sour the calibration and your relationships.

questions.question: A strong performer demands a 'Director' title to match a peer, with no change in scope or responsibility. Their leader wants to grant it to keep them happy, despite it distorting the levelling framework.

options.text: Tell the leader that handing out a title with no scope behind it devalues the level for everyone who earned it, and decline to support title inflation — offering to address the real retention concern instead.

Refer the request to the job-levelling framework and decline, on the basis that the role does not meet the documented criteria for a Director title regardless of the individual's performance or wishes.

Approve the title to keep a valued person happy, since it costs no salary and a small flexibility on titles is a cheap way to retain someone the leader clearly rates and genuinely wants to hold on to.

Ask for a full role-scope evaluation against the next level before deciding, deferring the question until a formal levelling assessment can establish whether the title could ever be justified later on.

questions.question: To save cost, leadership proposes cutting a benefit that matters most to the lowest-paid staff while leaving executive perks untouched. They frame it as a pragmatic, easily-absorbed saving.

options.text: Ask for a full impact analysis across pay levels before commenting, so any view you offer rests on a complete picture of who is affected and by how much rather than on an instinctive fairness reaction.
Raise the fairness concern with the CHRO and reward committee to decide, since how the cost of a benefit cut is distributed across levels is a policy matter above the HRBP to weigh in on alone.

Challenge the proposal directly, name that loading the cut onto those least able to absorb it while sparing exec perks is indefensible, and push for a fairer distribution of the saving across all levels.
Support the saving as proposed since leadership has decided, and focus on communicating the change to the affected staff as positively and supportively as the circumstances will reasonably allow.

questions.question: To land an external hire, the business agrees a large sign-on bonus and above-band salary. Existing loyal staff doing the same job earn considerably less, and a long-serving employee is starting to ask questions.

options.text: Point to the policy distinguishing new-hire market offers from internal pay reviews and treat them as separate, on the basis that the rules permit the external offer without touching anyone's internal pay.

Coach managers on holding the line if existing staff ask, framing the new package as market-driven, so the hire goes through smoothly and leadership avoids the cost of revisiting the wider team's pay.

Suggest scheduling a full internal benchmarking exercise at some future point to look at the team's pay, deferring the inequity to a later review rather than confronting the gap the hire has created now.

Tell leadership the internal inequity is real and will erode trust the moment it surfaces, and push them to address the existing team's pay rather than hope the gap stays hidden behind the new hire's package.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of giving employees a total reward statement?

options.text: To provide a complete, itemised record of every component of an employee's reward in a standardised format, so the organisation can demonstrate it has fully and accurately accounted for each element.
To give the organisation a data tool for analysing the cost and composition of reward across the workforce, so future reward decisions can be modelled against a comprehensive baseline of total spend.

To make employees feel more positively about their package by highlighting every element of value, so that satisfaction with reward improves and people are less likely to fixate only on base salary alone.

To show the full value an employee receives beyond base pay honestly — which only builds trust if the statement is candid rather than inflated to make a modest package look more generous than it really is.

questions.question: A leader wants to assign a low performance rating mainly so they can withhold a pay increase the employee is contractually due, rather than because the rating reflects the employee's actual performance.

options.text: Ask the leader to fully evidence the low rating before it's finalised, deferring the pay question until the documentation is complete rather than addressing the misuse of the rating directly with them now.
Help the leader frame the low rating defensibly so the pay outcome they want is supportable on paper, since they've decided the increase isn't warranted and your role is to make their decision stick.
Apply the rating-and-pay matrix as written once the leader records the rating, treating the mechanical link between rating and increase as resolving the question regardless of why the rating was set.

Refuse to let the rating be used as a device to avoid a contractual obligation, tell the leader plainly it's both dishonest and legally exposed, and insist the rating reflect genuine performance only.

questions.question: A long-term incentive scheme is clearly rewarding short-term metric gaming that harms the business, but fixing it would reduce the sponsoring leader's own payout. They resist any change to the design.

options.text: Name the conflict of interest openly and make the case to redesign the scheme on the evidence of harm, holding the position even though it cuts the leader's payout and they are the one resisting the change.

Propose only minor tweaks that leave the leader's payout largely intact, since pushing a redesign that hits their own reward would be politically costly and a gradual, low-friction adjustment is more realistic.

Commission an extended review of the scheme's effects over more cycles before recommending change, so any redesign rests on an unarguable evidence base and isn't seen as reacting to a single bad outcome.

Hand the design flaw to the remuneration committee and step back, so you avoid being the one who has to make the uncomfortable case to a leader who personally benefits from leaving the scheme unchanged.

questions.question: A cost-saving package cuts the benefits that matter most to lower-paid staff — shift allowances, hardship funds — while leaving executive perks untouched. Leadership wants HR to present it as 'shared sacrifice'.

options.text: Refuse to badge it as shared sacrifice when it isn't, tell leadership the unfairness will rightly anger people, and push for cuts that genuinely start at the top before they ever reach the lowest paid.

Propose an impact analysis across pay levels before communicating anything, so the distribution of the cuts is fully understood and the message can be refined once every effect has been carefully modelled.

Help craft the 'shared sacrifice' messaging as asked, since the savings are decided and your role is to land a difficult change as smoothly as possible while keeping leadership confident in HR's support.

Raise the fairness concern with the CHRO and reward committee and ask them to decide how to position it, since how cost cuts are distributed and framed is a decision above the HRBP to make on its own.

questions.question: A clearly high-potential employee is paid well below peers doing the same work. Their manager is content to leave it because the employee never complains and 'isn't a flight risk'.

options.text: Confirm the employee's pay falls within the approved range for their grade and treat that as sufficient, since the structure permits the figure and there's no policy breach requiring any adjustment be made.

Suggest reviewing the full peer-pay data and flight-risk indicators before recommending a change, so any uplift is fully justified by analysis rather than acted on before the case is firmly established.

Leave the pay as it is for now to avoid friction with the manager, and keep an eye on things in case the employee ever raises it, since they seem settled and there's no immediate pressure forcing a change.

Tell the manager that exploiting someone's silence to underpay them is unfair and short-sighted, and push to correct the gap proactively rather than waiting for them to threaten to leave before doing anything.

questions.question: What is the most important limitation for an HR leader to keep in mind when using external market benchmarking data?

options.text: That benchmarks must be matched precisely to job size, location and sector, since the value of the data depends entirely on the rigour with which roles are mapped to the relevant survey cuts and bands.

That benchmarks describe what the market does, not what is fair — leaning on them uncritically can launder existing inequities, and an HR leader must be willing to question the data rather than simply follow it.

That benchmarks need large, current datasets to be reliable, so decisions should wait until enough robust market evidence is gathered to ensure no pay position is ever taken on incomplete information.

That benchmarks should be applied generously enough to stay competitive, since paying below the market quartile risks losing the talent the business wants and creates dissatisfaction among those who compare.

questions.question: A promotion is open. A long-serving employee expects it 'because they've waited their turn', but a shorter-tenured colleague is clearly the stronger candidate. The leader wants to give it to the long-server to avoid upset.

options.text: Refer the contested promotion to a panel or your HR director to decide, since choosing between a loyal long-server and a stronger newcomer is a sensitive call best owned above you so the fallout doesn't land on HR.

Support giving it to the long-serving employee to keep the peace, since rewarding loyalty matters, the wait has built an expectation, and disappointing them risks a morale problem the leader wants to avoid.

Suggest gathering more assessment evidence on both before deciding, since a contested promotion needs a watertight case, and deferring lets the comparison be grounded fully before anyone is disappointed.

Tell the leader plainly that promoting on tenure over capability is unfair to the stronger candidate and signals the wrong thing to everyone, and make the case for the appointment to go on merit despite the upset.

questions.question: A strong performer wants an internal move that would clearly grow their career. Their current leader blocks it, citing 'business need' but really because they don't want to lose them. The employee is frustrated.

options.text: Point to the internal-mobility policy and its notice and approval requirements and process the move only if every condition is met, treating strict adherence to the procedure as resolving the disagreement.

Support the leader's decision to retain the employee for now, since they have a genuine resourcing need and overriding a leader's call on their own team's staffing would damage your working relationship.

Challenge the block openly, name that hoarding talent to suit one team harms the person and the company, and advocate for the move — telling the leader that keeping people by trapping them backfires every time.

Suggest reviewing the team's resourcing and pipeline before taking a view, so any recommendation on the move is grounded in a full picture of the business impact rather than just the employee's wish to grow.

questions.question: A leader wants to cut the pay of employees who've chosen to work remotely — framed as a 'location adjustment' but really to pressure them back to the office. Their performance is entirely unaffected.

options.text: Help design a defensible 'location-based pay' rationale that delivers what the leader wants, since they're set on driving office attendance and your job is to give them a workable mechanism to achieve it.

Check whether a location-based pay policy exists or can be applied and implement it if the framework allows, treating the existence of a permissible mechanism as settling whether the cut should go ahead.

Propose modelling the legal, retention and engagement impact before acting, so the pay change is only made once every consequence has been fully analysed and the approach can be comprehensively justified.

Tell the leader plainly that dressing up a return-to-office stick as a pay adjustment is punitive and corrosive, and push them to make the office case on its merits rather than by penalising people's pay.

questions.question: Beyond cash, what is the most important thing total rewards contributes to an employee value proposition?

options.text: A clearly structured and consistently administered set of reward components, so every employee understands precisely what they are entitled to and the offering is applied uniformly across the whole workforce.

A credible signal of what the organisation actually values — which means HR must ensure the rewards match the stated values, not let a generous-sounding package paper over a culture that contradicts it.

A comprehensive, attractive package of benefits and perks that makes employees feel well looked after, so the organisation is experienced as a generous employer that people are happy to recommend to others.

A well-benchmarked mix of pay and benefits supported by market data, so the organisation can demonstrate its package is competitive and defensible against any external comparison employees might make.

questions.question: A development budget was promised to a team to address skills gaps and was central to persuading them to take on more. Mid-year a leader quietly redirects it elsewhere and wants nothing said to the team.

options.text: Refer the decision to redirect a committed budget to your finance partner and HR director, since reversing a promise the team relied on is a call that should be owned above you rather than managed quietly.

Suggest waiting to see whether the budget can be reinstated later in the year before telling anyone, since raising a cut that might be reversed could damage trust unnecessarily over a temporary reallocation.

Push back on diverting a budget the team was promised and told to plan around, and insist they be told the truth if it truly can't be restored — telling the leader silent broken promises cost more than they save.

Help the leader reallocate quietly and find low-cost development alternatives to offer the team, since the business has shifting priorities and smooth handling avoids the disappointment of an open conversation.

questions.question: A manager wants to place an employee on a performance plan now — just before bonus determination — mainly so the 'under review' status disqualifies them from a bonus they would otherwise clearly have earned.

options.text: Help the manager initiate the plan in a way that's procedurally clean, since they clearly have concerns about the employee and your role is to support the manager in managing their own team's performance.

Refuse to let a performance process be weaponised to strip a deserved bonus, tell the manager plainly that's bad faith and exposes the company, and require any genuine concern be handled honestly on its own timeline.

Confirm the policy does suspend bonus eligibility during a formal performance plan and apply it, treating the rule's existence as settling the matter regardless of the manager's reason for the timing of it.

Ask the manager to document the performance concerns thoroughly before initiating anything, deferring the question of the suspicious timing rather than naming the misuse of the process directly with them now.

questions.question: A deferred-bonus clawback is being invoked against one departing employee on a technicality, while others who left in similar circumstances were never pursued. A leader wants it enforced to 'make a point'.

options.text: Confirm the clawback clause is contractually valid and apply it as written, treating the existence of the contractual right as the complete basis for enforcement regardless of how it was used in past cases.

Challenge the selective enforcement, name that punishing one person for what others did freely is unfair and legally risky, and insist the clawback be applied consistently or not used to settle a personal score.

Refer the selective enforcement question to Legal before acting, since invoking a clawback inconsistently carries litigation risk that should be assessed above the HRBP before any action is taken on it.

Enforce the clause as the leader wants, since the contract allows it and backing their decision on a departing employee keeps you aligned with them and avoids a confrontation over a single individual's exit.

questions.question: You've backed a reward decision you believe is fair — modest, evidence-based increases — but a vocal group is angry, and a manager is privately encouraging the backlash to get the pool reopened.

options.text: Hold off engaging until the anger settles and gather more data on the affected pay positions, so that any response you give rests on a complete picture rather than reacting to the heat of the moment right now.

Stand behind the decision openly, explain the rationale honestly to the team, and address the manager directly about undermining a fair outcome from behind the scenes — even though it makes you the target of the anger.

Look for a way to sweeten the outcome for the loudest group to defuse the anger, since calming a vocal backlash quickly protects morale and avoids the decision becoming a prolonged source of friction for everyone.

Pass the backlash up to your HR director and reward lead to handle, since a contested pay round with a manager stirring dissent is a situation best managed above your level rather than carried by you alone.

questions.question: An employee with a reputation for being 'difficult' raises a credible race-discrimination complaint. A leader wants to treat it as more of the same and not investigate, saying the person 'always has a grievance'.

options.text: Handle it informally to keep the leader onside, since they know the person's history and a light-touch approach avoids inflaming someone who tends to escalate while keeping the whole situation manageable.

Insist the complaint be investigated on its merits whatever the complainant's reputation, and tell the leader plainly that ignoring a credible discrimination claim because the person is 'difficult' is itself a serious risk.
Log the complaint and direct the employee to the formal grievance portal, treating pointing them to the proper channel as the appropriate response without engaging with the substance of it yourself.

Assess the complainant's track record and the credibility of the claim before deciding whether to investigate, since a long pattern of grievances suggests real caution before committing to a formal process.

questions.question: To cut costs, a leader wants to reclassify a group of hourly staff as salaried 'managers' — with no real change in duties — so the company no longer has to pay them overtime. You doubt it is lawful.

options.text: Help structure the reclassification so it looks defensible on paper, since the leader is set on the saving and your job is to give the business a workable way to achieve the cost reduction it has decided on.

Tell the leader plainly that relabelling staff to dodge overtime they're owed is unlawful and will cost more in back-pay and claims than it saves, and refuse to implement a classification you believe is a sham.

Refer the proposal to Legal and Finance to rule on, since reclassifying staff to change overtime liability is a decision with legal and financial exposure that should be owned above the HRBP rather than by you.

Commission legal advice on the exact classification tests before doing anything, since employment status is complex and you want full certainty about the boundaries before recommending for or against the change.

questions.question: Routine systems monitoring, set up for security, has incidentally captured an employee doing something a leader now wants to use to discipline them. The original purpose had nothing to do with conduct.

options.text: Find a way to use the evidence discreetly so the leader can act on what they've seen, since the behaviour is clearly a problem and being seen to help them deal with it keeps you valuable in their eyes.

Pass the question to Legal and Data Protection to decide whether the evidence can be used, since repurposing monitoring data carries compliance risk that should be ruled on above your level before any action.

Tell the leader you can't repurpose security-monitoring data to discipline someone, explain it breaches data-protection principles and would taint any case, and set out a lawful route if there's a real concern.
