Point to the monitoring policy's stated purpose and decline to release the data, treating the fact that the policy doesn't list discipline as the complete answer without addressing the wider judgement involved.

questions.question: A leader is desperate to onboard a key hire whose right-to-work documents look questionable. They tell you to 'just get them started' and not delay the start date over paperwork that's 'probably fine'.

options.text: Hold off engaging with the leader's pressure and ask for additional documentation before forming a view, since right-to-work questions are sensitive and you want to be fully certain before raising any concern.

Refuse to onboard anyone without valid right-to-work checks, tell the leader plainly that skipping them risks criminal liability for the company, and complete the checks properly before any start date is set.

Process the documents through the standard right-to-work checklist and onboard them if the boxes can be ticked, treating completion of the form as sufficient even where the documents themselves look doubtful.

Start them provisionally while the documents are 'sorted out', since the leader urgently needs the hire and a flexible approach gets the business moving while the paperwork is quietly tidied up in the background.

questions.question: A manager wants to quietly performance-manage out an employee who recently raised a protected disclosure about safety failings. There were no performance concerns at all before the disclosure was made.

options.text: Stop the move and tell the manager plainly that managing out a whistleblower with no prior concerns is unlawful retaliation that exposes the company, and that the disclosure must be handled on its own merits.

Suggest the manager build a careful, well-documented performance case first so any exit looks legitimate, since they clearly want this person gone and a defensible process protects them from obvious accusations.

Refer the situation to Legal and the CHRO before anything proceeds, since a potential whistleblower-retaliation claim is high-risk and should be assessed and owned above the HRBP rather than handled by you.

Ask the manager to gather detailed evidence of the performance concerns over the coming months before acting, deferring the question of the timing rather than naming the retaliation risk directly with them now.

questions.question: A serious near-miss on the production floor was quietly not reported, because logging it would trigger a shutdown and miss a key delivery. A leader wants it kept off the record 'since no one was actually hurt'.

options.text: Log the incident in the safety system as the procedure requires and consider the obligation discharged, treating the act of recording it as sufficient without pushing for the investigation or shutdown it warrants.

Suggest reviewing the severity of the near-miss and the reporting thresholds before deciding whether it must be logged, since not every event meets the bar and you want certainty before forcing a costly shutdown.

Note the concern but go along with keeping it quiet this once, since no one was hurt, the delivery is critical, and pressing the issue would put you at odds with a leader under real pressure to perform.

Insist the near-miss be reported and investigated, telling the leader that hiding it to protect a delivery risks the next incident being a real injury — and accept the disruption that honest reporting brings.

questions.question: What is the core purpose a grievance procedure is meant to serve in an organisation?

options.text: To set out a consistent, documented sequence of steps that must be followed in every case, so the organisation can demonstrate procedural correctness and protect itself if a decision is later challenged.

To give employees a fair, impartial route to have genuine concerns heard and acted on — its value lies in HR ensuring it delivers real justice, not in the existence of the document or the ticking of its steps.

To provide a formal channel through which concerns can be thoroughly examined and evidenced before any conclusion is reached, ensuring no grievance is acted on until it has been fully and exhaustively assessed.

To give employees a structured way to feel heard so dissatisfaction is contained and resolved internally, keeping concerns from escalating into wider conflict or damaging relationships across the organisation.

questions.question: Several long-term 'contractors' work full-time under the same control and conditions as employees, but are kept off payroll to avoid tax and employment obligations. A leader wants to carry on exactly as-is.

options.text: Tell the leader plainly that the arrangement looks like disguised employment carrying real tax and legal exposure, and push to put genuine contractors on the right footing rather than maintaining a risky fiction.

Leave the arrangement undisturbed as the leader wishes, since it's worked for years, the contractors aren't complaining, and reopening their status would create cost and disruption the leader wants to avoid.

Refer the question of the contractors' status to Legal and Tax to rule on, since misclassification carries liabilities that should be assessed and owned above the HRBP rather than raised and acted on by you.

Commission a detailed status review of every contractor before raising anything, since employment-status tests are nuanced and you want complete certainty about each individual before challenging the model.

questions.question: Responding to an employee's data subject access request, a leader instructs you to quietly remove some unflattering internal emails about the employee before the file is disclosed to them.

options.text: Apply the standard exemptions in the data-protection guidance and withhold whatever can technically be withheld, treating maximal use of the exemptions as the proper way to limit what has to be disclosed.

Quietly leave out the most sensitive emails to spare the leader embarrassment, since the employee won't know what existed and a cleaner file avoids inflaming a situation the leader wants kept contained.

Refuse to remove or alter responsive records, tell the leader that tampering with a subject access response is unlawful and far more damaging than the emails themselves, and disclose what the law requires.

Suggest taking legal advice on which documents could be withheld before responding, deferring the disclosure deadline rather than confronting the leader's instruction to remove records directly and right now.

questions.question: To settle a harassment claim, a leader wants a settlement agreement with an NDA broad enough to stop the victim reporting the harasser's ongoing behaviour to regulators or warning future colleagues.

options.text: Refuse to draft an NDA that gags lawful reporting of ongoing wrongdoing, tell the leader it's likely unenforceable and ethically indefensible, and insist any settlement leave protected disclosures fully intact.

Use the company's standard settlement template with its usual confidentiality clauses and proceed, treating reliance on the established precedent wording as sufficient without examining what it actually suppresses.

Help craft the broadest agreement the leader wants to close the matter cleanly, since a comprehensive NDA protects the company's reputation and getting the claim settled quietly is what the leader is asking for.

Refer the scope of the NDA to Legal to decide on, since how far confidentiality can extend over reportable conduct is a legal question that should be ruled on above your level before the agreement is offered.

questions.question: How should an HR leader think about the relationship between what is legally permissible and what is the right thing to do?

options.text: Treat the law as the floor, not the ceiling, and be willing to argue against an action that's technically lawful but unfair — rather than using 'it's legal' to avoid the harder judgement about what is right.

Ensure every decision is fully compliant with the relevant legislation and policy, since an organisation that rigorously follows the rules in all cases has met its obligations and protected itself from any risk.

Stay within the law while giving leaders as much room as possible to do what they want, since HR adds most value by enabling the business and finding ways to say yes rather than acting as a constraint on it.

Seek legal clarity on the permissibility of any contested action before forming a view, since the right course can only be judged once the precise legal position has been fully and definitively established.

questions.question: A leader wants to dismiss an employee on long-term sick leave with a disability, skipping occupational-health input and adjustments, because the absence is 'going on too long' and covering it is expensive.

options.text: Apply the absence-management policy's trigger points and proceed once they're reached, treating the fact that the thresholds are met as justifying dismissal without the occupational-health and adjustment steps.

Help the leader build a route to exit the employee that looks defensible, since the absence is genuinely costly and your role is to find the business a workable way through a situation that's dragged on too long.

Refuse to support a dismissal that skips occupational health and adjustments, tell the leader it's discriminatory and unsafe, and insist on the proper process even though it means living with the cost a while longer.

Suggest commissioning further medical evidence and reviews before any decision, deferring the matter through repeated assessment rather than either supporting or challenging the leader's wish to move now.

questions.question: A part of the business is being outsourced and the affected staff have legal rights to be informed and consulted about the transfer. A leader wants to skip consultation to keep the deal quiet until it's signed.

options.text: Commission legal advice on the precise consultation obligations before raising anything, since transfer rules are technical and you want full certainty about what's required before challenging the leader's plan.

Refer the consultation question to Legal and the deal team to own, since obligations on a transfer carry liability that should be assessed and decided above the HRBP rather than raised and enforced by you.

Tell the leader plainly that failing to inform and consult on a transfer breaches the law and triggers penalties per employee, and insist consultation happen properly even if it means the deal can't stay secret.

Help keep the transfer confidential as the leader wants and plan a brief, late consultation just before signing, since protecting the deal's secrecy is the priority and a light process limits the disruption.

questions.question: Angry that a good employee is leaving for a competitor, a leader wants you to give the new employer a damning, exaggerated reference to sabotage the move and 'teach them a lesson'.

options.text: Soften the reference just enough to subtly signal concerns without being overtly negative, giving the leader the discouraging effect they want while keeping the wording deniable enough to avoid a clear problem.

Refuse to give a false or exaggerated reference, tell the leader plainly that a malicious reference is defamatory and exposes the company, and provide only an accurate, factual account of the employee's record.

Apply the references policy limiting the company to dates and job title and decline to say more, treating the bare policy as the answer without addressing the leader's improper intent behind the request itself.

Suggest delaying any reference until the employee's full record is reviewed, deferring the request rather than confronting the leader's plan to sabotage the move with a reference you know would be untrue.

questions.question: A leader wants to give a newly hired man a large 'market premium' that would put him well above a woman doing equal work of equal value who has been there for years. They see no problem with it at all.

options.text: Approve the premium to land the hire, since the market is what it is and the leader needs the candidate — and the existing employee's pay can be quietly looked at later if she ever happens to raise the point.

Flag the equal-pay risk directly, tell the leader that paying a man more than a woman for equal work without a genuine material-factor justification invites a claim, and require the gap be justified or closed.

Ask for a full equal-pay analysis across the comparator group before raising it, since an equal-value claim is complex and you want certainty the roles truly are equal before challenging the proposed premium.

Confirm the premium sits within the pay range for the grade and approve it on that basis, treating compliance with the band as sufficient without examining the equal-pay exposure the comparison clearly creates.

questions.question: A leader has already decided to dismiss an employee and tells you to 'just run the hearing' as a formality to reach that outcome. The evidence is genuinely contested and the case is far from clear-cut.

options.text: Follow each documented step of the disciplinary procedure and record them carefully, treating completion of the prescribed steps as making the dismissal sound regardless of whether the outcome was decided first.

Refer the predetermination concern to your HR director before the hearing, since running a process with a fixed outcome carries unfair-dismissal risk that should be ruled on above your level rather than by you.

Run a procedurally tidy hearing that arrives where the leader wants, since they've made their call and your role is to ensure the process looks correct and protects them from an obvious procedural challenge.

Refuse to run a hearing with a predetermined outcome, tell the leader that a sham process makes any dismissal unfair and indefensible, and insist the hearing be genuinely open to the evidence either way.

questions.question: What standard of proof applies when deciding the outcome of a workplace misconduct investigation?

options.text: Whatever level of certainty lets the organisation reach an outcome people can accept, since an investigation that leaves everyone comfortable and avoids appeals has effectively served its purpose for the business.

The standard set out in the disciplinary policy, applied exactly as written, since adhering precisely to the documented evidential threshold is what makes the investigation's conclusion procedurally correct.

A high degree of certainty supported by comprehensive evidence, since a finding should only be made once every reasonable doubt has been eliminated and the case is effectively beyond any serious challenge.

The balance of probabilities — and HR's job is to hold decision-makers to applying it honestly, neither demanding criminal-level certainty to avoid a hard finding nor lowering the bar to reach a convenient one.

questions.question: You learn a hiring manager has been accepting expensive gifts and trips from a recruitment agency they keep awarding contracts to. The manager is well-connected and shrugs it off as 'just relationship building'.

options.text: Refer the matter straight to Compliance and Legal without speaking to the manager, since gifts from a supplier are a governance issue best handled formally from above rather than raised by you directly.

Name it for what it is — a conflict of interest and likely bribery risk — tell the manager it must stop and be declared, and ensure it's reported through the proper channel regardless of how well-connected they are.

Have a quiet word suggesting they ease off the gifts, but avoid formalising anything, since the manager is influential, the agency performs well, and making it official would create friction you'd rather avoid.

Gather more detail on the value and frequency of the gifts before deciding whether to act, since an accusation of this kind needs to be firmly evidenced before you raise it about a well-connected manager.

questions.question: Demand is high and a leader wants safety-critical staff to keep working through legally required rest breaks to hit output. They argue the team has agreed to it and 'everyone is fine'.

options.text: Allow a temporary arrangement since the team has agreed and demand is high, monitoring it informally, since the staff are willing and pushing back hard would put you at odds with a leader under real pressure.

Have the staff sign waivers and record their consent, treating documented agreement as resolving the issue without addressing whether working through rest in safety-critical roles is actually permissible at all.

Refuse to set aside mandatory rest for safety-critical roles, tell the leader that fatigue in these jobs endangers people and breaches the law regardless of any 'agreement', and insist the breaks be protected.

Review the working-time rules and the roles affected before responding, since the limits and exceptions are technical and you want full certainty about what's allowed before challenging the leader's plan.

questions.question: An HR error has exposed a file of employees' personal data externally. A leader wants to fix it quietly and not report it to the regulator or the affected people, to avoid 'making a fuss over something minor'.

options.text: Insist the breach be assessed and reported as the law requires and the affected people told, telling the leader that concealing a notifiable breach is a far bigger risk than the breach itself ever was.

Help contain it quietly and hold off on any reporting, since the exposure may be limited, and avoiding a regulator notification spares the organisation embarrassment and the leader the difficult conversations.

Pass the breach to Legal and the data-protection officer to decide on reporting and then step back, since whether a breach is notifiable is a judgement that should be made above your level before anyone is informed.

Log the incident in the breach register and apply the standard remediation steps, treating the act of recording and patching it as discharging the obligation without making the notification assessment required.

questions.question: What does the duty to make reasonable adjustments for a disabled employee actually require of an employer?

options.text: Assessing each request through thorough medical and cost-benefit analysis before agreeing anything, ensuring no adjustment is committed to until its necessity and proportionality are completely established first.

Following the documented adjustments procedure and providing whatever it specifies once occupational health has reported, treating completion of the process as fully discharging the employer's duty in every case.

A genuine, active effort to remove the disadvantage the disability creates — which means HR pushing leaders to do what genuinely helps the person, not just whatever is cheapest or least disruptive for the business.

Providing adjustments that keep the employee content and supported while remaining acceptable to managers, so the person feels looked after and the arrangement doesn't create resentment among their colleagues.

questions.question: A redundancy is being engineered so the selection criteria — weighted heavily on a skill only the target lacks — guarantee one specific person is chosen, while it's dressed up as an objective process.

options.text: Ask for the rationale behind each criterion and its weighting before raising concerns, since challenging a selection method needs solid evidence that it was engineered rather than genuinely role-driven first.

Challenge the rigged criteria directly, name that designing selection to pre-determine one person's exit is unfair dismissal waiting to happen, and require genuinely objective, role-relevant criteria be used.

Check the selection matrix is completed and scored consistently and approve it on that basis, treating the existence of a filled-in, internally consistent matrix as confirming the process is objective and fair.

Help refine the criteria so the process looks defensible, since the leader clearly needs this person out and your role is to give the business a selection method that achieves the outcome without obvious flaws.

questions.question: Evidence emerges that a labour provider supplying your warehouse is exploiting workers — withheld wages, confiscated documents. A leader wants to quietly switch providers without reporting or investigating it.

options.text: Arrange a discreet switch to a new provider as the leader prefers, since ending the relationship solves the immediate problem and avoiding a formal report spares the company exposure and difficult scrutiny.

Insist the exploitation be investigated and reported through the proper channels, not just quietly dropped, telling the leader that looking away from likely modern slavery is both unlawful and morally indefensible.

Gather more evidence to confirm the exploitation is real and systemic before raising it formally, since an allegation this serious about a supplier needs to be firmly substantiated before any action is taken.

Refer the matter to Legal and Procurement to handle, since exploitation in the supply chain raises legal and reporting obligations that sit well above the HRBP to assess and act on independently of them.

questions.question: A leader wants to dismiss an employee still in their probation period, citing a minor performance niggle. You know the leader only soured on her after she disclosed she was pregnant last week.

options.text: Stop the dismissal and tell the leader plainly that acting right after a pregnancy disclosure looks like discrimination whatever the probation status, and require any genuine concern be evidenced and handled fairly.

Ask the leader to document the performance concerns more fully before acting, deferring the decision rather than naming the obvious discrimination risk created by the timing of it directly with them now.

Help the leader frame the probation dismissal around the performance niggle so it looks clean, since probation gives them latitude and presenting it carefully avoids the awkward link to her recent disclosure.

Confirm the probation policy permits dismissal with short notice and process it on that basis, treating the contractual right to dismiss in probation as sufficient without weighing the timing and discrimination risk.

questions.question: An employee is leaving for a role you know falls outside any genuine competitive threat. A leader wants to threaten aggressive enforcement of a broad non-compete purely to intimidate them and deter others.

options.text: Point to the signed contract's restrictive covenant and confirm it can be cited, treating the existence of the clause as justifying the threat without assessing whether it's actually enforceable or proportionate.

Suggest taking legal advice on the covenant's enforceability before doing anything, deferring the question rather than challenging the leader's intent to use it as intimidation regardless of its real strength.

Advise against brandishing an unenforceable covenant as a threat, tell the leader that intimidating a leaver with a clause you can't defend invites a backlash and looks vindictive, and handle the exit professionally.

Send a firm letter reminding the employee of their obligations as the leader wants, since a show of strength may deter others from leaving and backing the leader's stance keeps you firmly aligned with them.

questions.question: An HR leader should be able to distinguish discrimination, harassment and victimisation. What most matters about getting these distinctions right in practice?

options.text: That careful analysis of the facts against each definition prevents premature labelling, so no conduct is formally characterised until the evidence has been fully examined and the correct category established.

That naming the right wrong drives the right response — and HR must be willing to call conduct what it actually is, including victimisation of someone who complained, rather than downgrading it to keep things calm.

That each term maps to a precise statutory definition which must be applied exactly, since correctly categorising conduct against the legal tests is what ensures the organisation's response is procedurally sound.

That using the correct terms reassures employees their concerns are taken seriously, helping maintain trust and a positive climate so issues are resolved internally without escalating into formal disputes or claims.

questions.question: For a sensitive grievance, a leader wants to appoint an investigator known to be close to the accused manager and likely to reach a 'sensible' outcome, rather than someone genuinely impartial.

options.text: Insist on an impartial investigator, telling the leader plainly that appointing someone aligned with the accused destroys the process's credibility and any finding it produces, whatever the convenience of the choice.

Confirm the proposed investigator meets the formal criteria in the policy and appoint them, treating satisfaction of the documented requirements as sufficient without weighing the actual conflict of interest here.

Go along with the suggested investigator to keep the leader comfortable, since they know the people involved and a familiar, pragmatic investigator is likely to handle a sensitive matter with the least disruption.

Suggest assessing several candidates' suitability in detail before appointing anyone, deferring the decision rather than directly challenging the leader's clear preference for an investigator who isn't impartial.

questions.question: A leader wants to suspend an employee without pay during an investigation as a way of pressuring them, even though the contract provides for suspension on full pay and there's no basis to withhold it.

options.text: Find a way to frame a reduced payment that partly satisfies the leader's wish to apply pressure, since they feel strongly about the case and a compromise avoids an open clash over how the suspension is handled.

Suggest seeking legal advice on whether pay can be withheld before acting, deferring the matter rather than telling the leader directly that using unpaid suspension to pressure the employee is unlawful here.

Check whether any policy provision could support withholding pay and apply it if one exists, treating the search for a technical basis as the task rather than questioning the leader's intent to use pay as pressure.

Refuse to withhold pay there's no basis to withhold, tell the leader that unpaid suspension here is an unlawful deduction and prejudges guilt, and ensure the employee is suspended on full pay as the contract requires.

questions.question: An employee requests a small schedule adjustment to observe a religious obligation. It's easily accommodated, but a leader wants to refuse it as a matter of 'not setting a precedent' and keeping rotas simple.

options.text: Look for a low-key informal workaround that gives the employee what they need without the leader having to formally agree anything, so neither the precedent worry nor the employee's request becomes an open issue.

Apply the flexible-working and scheduling policy and refuse if the request falls outside its standard provisions, treating the policy's silence on this situation as a sufficient basis to decline the adjustment.

Tell the leader plainly that refusing an easily-met religious request to avoid a 'precedent' is indirect discrimination, and press them to accommodate it — the minor rota effort doesn't justify the legal and human cost.

Ask the employee to set out the religious obligation and its frequency in detail before deciding, deferring the simple accommodation through process rather than challenging the leader's reluctance to grant it.

questions.question: An employee, having exhausted internal channels with no action, tells you they intend to report a genuine safety cover-up to the regulator. A leader wants you to dissuade them and warn them off.

options.text: Have a careful conversation encouraging the employee to give the company more time internally, since keeping the matter in-house protects the organisation and steering them away keeps the leader reassured.

Decline to warn them off, tell the leader that a protected external disclosure can't be suppressed and that the real fix is addressing the cover-up — and ensure the employee knows their rights are protected.

Refer the situation to Legal to manage the employee's intended disclosure, since an external report to a regulator carries serious exposure that should be handled above the HRBP rather than by you directly.

Remind the employee of the confidentiality clauses in their contract and the internal-reporting policy, treating pointing to those provisions as the appropriate response without addressing the underlying cover-up.

questions.question: What distinguishes good HR record-keeping from record-keeping that exists mainly to protect the organisation?

options.text: Records framed to keep relationships positive and avoid putting anything in writing that could upset a manager or employee, so the documentation supports a constructive climate rather than creating friction.

A complete, consistently formatted file with every required form and signature in place, since thorough adherence to the documentation standard is what demonstrates the process was correctly followed throughout.

Extensive, detailed records of every interaction and consideration, so that no decision can ever be questioned for lack of evidence and any challenge can be met with an exhaustive documentary trail of it.

Honest records that capture what actually happened, including facts inconvenient for the company — HR's integrity shows in documenting the truth, not in curating a file built only to defend a chosen position.

questions.question: A restructure will make more than enough people redundant to trigger collective consultation. A leader wants to break it into smaller 'waves' specifically to slip under the threshold and skip consultation.

options.text: Commission legal advice on exactly how the thresholds and aggregation rules apply before raising it, since collective consultation rules are intricate and you want certainty before challenging the leader's plan.

Help design the phased approach so each wave stays under the threshold, since avoiding the time and cost of collective consultation is what the leader wants and the structure technically keeps each round small.

Refer the threshold question to Legal and the project board to decide, since whether consultation is triggered carries liability that should be assessed and owned above the HRBP rather than determined by you.

Tell the leader plainly that artificially splitting redundancies to dodge collective consultation is unlawful and well-known to tribunals, and insist the obligations be met properly rather than engineered away.

questions.question: An employee needs an adjustment that has a modest cost. A leader wants to tell them they can have it only if they pay for it themselves or otherwise go without, to keep it off the department's budget.

options.text: Suggest a cost-benefit review of the adjustment before agreeing how it's funded, deferring the question rather than telling the leader directly that asking the employee to pay is simply not a lawful option here.

Tell the leader plainly that making a disabled employee fund their own reasonable adjustment is unlawful and unacceptable, and ensure the cost is met by the employer as the duty clearly requires it to be.

Look for a cheaper alternative that keeps the leader's budget concern satisfied while giving the employee something, even if it's not quite what they need, so the cost worry and the request are both partly met.

Check whether the cost falls within the adjustments budget and refuse if it doesn't, treating the budget position as determining the outcome without addressing the legal duty to fund the adjustment regardless.

questions.question: A strong internal candidate disclosed a minor, long-spent conviction unrelated to the role. A leader wants to quietly drop them from the process over it, despite it being legally irrelevant to this position.

options.text: Tell the leader the spent conviction is legally irrelevant here and can't drive the decision, and insist the candidate be assessed on merit — naming that rejecting them over it would be both unlawful and unfair.

Find another plausible reason to ease the candidate out of the process, giving the leader the outcome they want without the rejection being visibly tied to the conviction they're uncomfortable about at all.

Apply the recruitment screening policy and check whether the conviction triggers any documented bar, treating the outcome of the checklist as the answer without addressing whether it's lawful to consider it here.

Suggest reviewing the relevance of the conviction and the legal position before advising, deferring the decision rather than telling the leader plainly that it cannot lawfully be held against the candidate now.

questions.question: After a viral customer complaint, leadership wants an employee dismissed by the end of the day to be seen acting decisively. The facts are unclear and a fair process would take longer. You're the only one urging caution.

options.text: Propose gathering all the facts through a full investigation before any decision, focusing on the process while avoiding directly telling leadership that the dismissal they want today would be unfair and risky.

Hold the line for a fair process, tell leadership plainly that a knee-jerk dismissal on unclear facts is unfair and exposes the company far more than a short delay, and accept being the unpopular voice in the room.

Help arrange the swift dismissal leadership wants while documenting it as carefully as time allows, since the reputational pressure is real and being seen to enable decisive action keeps you aligned with the room.

Push the decision up to the CHRO and Legal to make the call, since a high-profile dismissal under public pressure is too sensitive for the HRBP to own and should be decided at the most senior level instead.

questions.question: Your analysis shows a flagship initiative the CHRO champions is failing. They ask you to soften the findings before the board sees them, so the programme keeps its funding and their credibility holds.

options.text: Reframe the findings to be more balanced and hopeful as the CHRO asks, since they've backed this initiative publicly and supporting your boss's position is part of being a reliable, trusted member of their team.

Present the findings honestly to the board, tell the CHRO directly you can't doctor your own analysis to protect a programme that isn't working, and accept the personal cost of contradicting the person you report to.

Suggest commissioning further analysis over the next quarter before presenting anything definitive, since a single read on a complex programme warrants more data before you put damaging conclusions to the board.

Present only the metrics the reporting template strictly requires and leave interpretation to others, treating bare adherence to the standard report format as discharging your duty without drawing the hard conclusion.

questions.question: You realise a significant error you made months ago is about to cause a visible problem. No one has connected it to you yet, and you could quietly fix it or let it be blamed on a process gap.

options.text: Raise it as a 'process failure' for leadership to review systemically, framing it as an organisational learning point so the focus stays on fixing the system rather than on identifying who was actually responsible.

Gather full detail on exactly how the error arose and its consequences before saying anything, since you want to be completely sure of your own culpability before volunteering an admission that can't be taken back.

Own the error openly to the people affected and your leadership before it surfaces, explain what you'll do to fix it, and accept the hit to your reputation rather than let it land on a process or someone else.

Fix it quietly and let the 'process gap' explanation stand, since openly confessing serves little purpose now, the problem can be contained, and preserving how colleagues see you keeps you effective going forward.

questions.question: A junior on your team did the bulk of the work on a piece that's just won real praise from the executive team, who assume it was you. Correcting them costs you nothing tangible but a moment's spotlight.

options.text: Plan to acknowledge the junior at a more suitable moment later, since interrupting the executives' praise now would be awkward and you'd rather find the right setting to set the record straight properly.

Correct the record clearly and name the junior's contribution to the executive team, since taking or even passively accepting credit that isn't yours undermines the very trust your own leadership depends on.

Note the junior's contribution in the project documentation and performance records as the process provides, treating proper internal recording of who did what as sufficient without correcting the executives directly.

Accept the praise graciously and mention the team generally, since singling out a junior in front of executives could seem like deflecting and a strong leader is expected to own their team's output anyway.

questions.question: In a leadership meeting, a peer is being blamed for a failure that was genuinely a shared decision you were part of. They're absent and can't defend themselves. Staying quiet would leave you untouched.

options.text: Hold back from intervening until you've spoken to the peer and confirmed the full sequence of events, since correcting the narrative needs to rest on a clear account rather than your own recollection of it.

Suggest the matter be reviewed properly outside the meeting with all parties present, deferring any defence of the peer to a formal setting rather than challenging the unfair blame while it's actually happening.

Speak up and set out that the decision was shared and you own your part, even though it draws blame onto you, since letting an absent peer carry a failure that was collective is something you can't be party to.

Stay measured and let the discussion move on without contradicting the room, since the peer can put their side later and openly reapportioning blame in the moment would only inflame a tense meeting unnecessarily.

questions.question: What does professional courage most genuinely require of a senior HR leader?

options.text: Being willing to say the uncomfortable thing and hold the position even when it's unpopular with powerful people and carries a personal cost — courage that evaporates the moment it's risky was never really courage.

The judgement to pick the right battles carefully and only take a stand once the evidence is overwhelming, so that when HR does push back it does so from an unassailable and fully defensible position.

The discipline to uphold policy and process consistently even when leaders would prefer exceptions, since standing firm on the rules in the face of pressure is how HR maintains its integrity and credibility.

The confidence to advocate strongly for people and for HR's agenda, so the function's voice is heard and respected and HR is seen as a bold, energetic partner rather than a passive administrative service.

questions.question: You argued hard against a major decision and lost; leadership has chosen a different path. Your team looks to you for how to respond, and you still believe the decision is wrong.

options.text: Stay neutral with your team and implement it without comment, avoiding either endorsing or criticising it, so you're not seen to own a call you opposed if it later turns out to have been the wrong one after all.

Get fully behind the decision and set your reservations aside entirely, since visibly supporting leadership keeps you aligned and a leader who keeps relitigating lost arguments loses influence and goodwill fast.

Commit to making the decision work and represent it honestly to your team, while keeping a clear record of your concerns — disagreeing openly beforehand and then backing the call is integrity, not capitulation.

Implement the decision exactly as mandated and refer any concerns to the formal change process, treating precise execution of the instruction as the limit of your responsibility now that the call has been made.

questions.question: Your boss asks you to help conceal a decision of theirs that you believe is against the organisation's interests, appealing to your loyalty and the support they've shown your career.

options.text: Tell your boss honestly that your loyalty can't extend to concealing something that harms the organisation, and that you'll have to act in the company's interest even though it strains a relationship you value.

Tell your boss you need to think it over and gather more facts, buying time in the hope the situation resolves itself or the decision changes before you're forced to choose between them and the organisation.

Help your boss as they've asked, since they've invested in you and stood by you, and loyalty to the person who has backed your career is itself a value worth honouring when they're under real pressure.

Take your concern straight to your boss's manager or the audit function without telling your boss, since a decision against the organisation's interests is a governance matter that belongs above you to resolve.

questions.question: Leadership wants you to front a company-wide announcement spinning a painful change as a positive 'opportunity for growth', in words you find misleading. Employees trust you and will believe you.

options.text: Suggest the announcement come from the CEO or comms instead of you, passing the uncomfortable delivery up or sideways rather than challenging the framing or owning your discomfort with the message itself.

Push back on the misleading framing and insist on being honest about the hard parts, telling leadership that trading the trust employees place in you for a spin that won't hold is a bad bargain for everyone.

Ask to delay the announcement while the messaging is refined, deferring your delivery rather than telling leadership directly that you won't put your credibility behind a framing you believe to be misleading.

Deliver the upbeat message as leadership wants but with as much warmth as you can, since your role is to land difficult changes positively and being the reassuring face of the announcement is part of the job.

questions.question: What should a senior HR leader prioritise in how they are perceived by the business?

options.text: Being seen as consistent and even-handed in applying the rules, since a reputation for reliable, predictable adherence to policy is what earns an HR leader the organisation's confidence over the long term.

Being regarded as measured and thorough, since an HR leader known for never acting without full evidence inspires confidence that decisions are sound and that the function won't expose the business to risk.

Being liked and approachable across the business, since strong relationships and goodwill are what give an HR leader the access and influence to get things done and to bring people along with them on change.

Being trusted and respected over being liked — because an HR leader who needs to be liked will eventually trade away hard truths to stay popular, and credibility comes from being the person who tells it straight.

questions.question: A powerful executive wants to use you and HR processes to build a case against a rival they dislike, framing it as you simply 'doing your job' and hinting it would be good for your standing with them.

options.text: Ask the executive to put their specific concerns in writing so you can assess them properly first, buying time and avoiding a direct refusal while hoping the matter loses momentum before you have to act on it.

Decline to be turned into a weapon against someone, tell the executive plainly that HR won't manufacture a case to settle their personal score, and accept the cost of disappointing a powerful would-be patron.

Refer the executive's concerns to your HR director to handle, since being asked to act against a rival by a powerful stakeholder is politically charged and best owned above you rather than navigated by yourself.

Engage helpfully with the executive's concerns and look into the rival, since staying close to a powerful sponsor is valuable and you can keep the process fair while still being responsive to what they want.

questions.question: A turnaround in your area is being celebrated and attributed to your leadership, but you privately know it was mostly external factors and a predecessor's groundwork. The praise is boosting your profile.

options.text: Plan to give a fuller, more nuanced account of the turnaround at a later review, since the moment of celebration isn't the right time and a measured correction can wait for a more appropriate setting later.

Accept the recognition modestly and let it stand, since the team and the business benefit from the positive story, and publicly dissecting why the praise is overstated would only undercut everyone's morale.

Ensure the contributing factors are accurately recorded in the formal review documentation, treating correct internal record-keeping as sufficient without actively correcting the narrative being celebrated openly.

Be candid about what actually drove the turnaround, including the external factors and your predecessor's work, since accepting inflated credit builds a reputation on sand and erodes your own integrity over time.

questions.question: A close friend in the business, anxious about rumoured restructuring, presses you for whatever you know — insisting it stays between you and that real friends would help each other in a moment like this.

options.text: Remind them of the confidentiality policy and that you can't discuss restructuring, treating the citation of the rule as the answer without acknowledging the friendship or the genuine distress they're feeling.

Give them a reassuring hint about their own situation to ease their anxiety, since they're a genuine friend in distress and a small, careful disclosure between trusted friends does little real harm to anyone.

Tell them you don't really know much yet and you'll let them know if anything firms up, deflecting with vagueness rather than being clear about the boundary so you avoid an uncomfortable moment with a friend.

Tell your friend honestly that you can't share confidential information even with them, and that compromising it would betray both your role and ultimately the trust that makes you worth confiding in at all.

questions.question: In a high-stakes meeting, a senior leader asks you a pointed question about a legal risk you're genuinely unsure of. A confident-sounding answer would land well; admitting uncertainty might look weak.

options.text: Say it's a complex area that needs careful analysis before you'd want to comment, framing the non-answer as diligence so you neither expose your uncertainty nor risk being wrong in front of the leader right now.

Refer the leader to the relevant policy and say the matter will be handled per process, treating deflection to the documented procedure as a safe response that avoids both committing and admitting uncertainty.

Say plainly you don't want to give a confident answer you're not sure of, and commit to coming back with a firm one quickly, since guessing on a legal risk to look strong is exactly how people get badly misled.

Give a confident, reassuring answer in the direction you think is right, since the leader wants decisiveness and projecting command keeps their faith in you — you can quietly correct the detail afterwards if needed.

questions.question: A call you made a year ago — unpopular at the time — is being second-guessed now that conditions have changed. You still believe it was right on the information you had, but caving would be easier.

options.text: Acknowledge the critics' view and distance yourself from the call, since conceding gracefully keeps relationships smooth and a leader who admits a decision looks dated wins more goodwill than one who defends it.

Suggest a full review of the decision and its context before responding to the criticism, deferring any defence until an analysis can establish definitively whether it was right rather than taking a position now.

Stand by the reasoning, explain the basis on which it was right at the time, and own it fully — while being open about what you'd do differently now, rather than disowning the decision just to look agile in hindsight.

Refer the questions to the governance forum that signed the decision off, framing it as a collective call for the committee to revisit rather than personally standing behind the judgement you actually made.

questions.question: What most distinguishes an HR leader who genuinely grows over their career from one who plateaus?

options.text: Keeping their technical knowledge and qualifications consistently up to date, since an HR leader who rigorously maintains their expertise and credentials stays credible and relevant as the profession evolves.

Carefully analysing each experience before drawing conclusions, since a leader who reflects thoroughly and avoids hasty self-judgement builds a more accurate and reliable picture of their own development over time.

Building a strong network of supportive relationships and mentors, since a leader who is well-connected and well-liked has the access, sponsorship and goodwill needed to keep advancing throughout their career.

Actively seeking out the feedback they least want to hear and acting on it — growth depends on being willing to confront your own blind spots, not on collecting reassurance that confirms what you already believe.

questions.question: You're offered ownership of a high-visibility programme that would supercharge your profile, but you can see it will pull resources from work the organisation actually needs more. Few would notice the trade-off.

options.text: Ask for more analysis of the resourcing trade-offs before committing either way, deferring the decision rather than openly naming that the visible programme is the worse use of resource for the organisation.

Take the high-visibility programme and make it a success, since a win here strengthens your standing and an influential HR leader can do more good overall from a position of greater profile and credibility.

Be honest that the organisation's greater need should win, and flag the trade-off openly even though it means turning down the spotlight, since quietly choosing your own profile over what the business needs is self-serving.

Accept the programme since it's been formally assigned to you through the proper governance, treating the legitimacy of the assignment process as settling the question without weighing the resourcing trade-off.

questions.question: You become certain your own manager is doing something clearly unethical — not a judgement call, but wrong. Confronting them risks your standing; they control your reviews and your progression.

options.text: Raise it with them directly and honestly first, and if it isn't addressed, take it where it needs to go — accepting the real risk to your position rather than staying silent about something you know is wrong.

Keep careful private notes and wait for clearer, more incontrovertible evidence before doing anything, since acting against your own manager on something this serious demands a case that simply cannot be disputed.

Find a tactful way to steer them away from it without a confrontation, since preserving the relationship with the person who controls your progression lets you keep influencing them positively over time.

Report it anonymously through the whistleblowing line and let the formal process take over, since misconduct by your manager is best handled through official channels that keep you out of a direct confrontation.

questions.question: A leadership team you're part of has quickly converged on a decision everyone seems comfortable with, but you can see a serious flaw no one is naming. Raising it would break a rare moment of easy consensus.

options.text: Let the consensus stand and raise your concern quietly with one or two people afterwards, since disrupting a rare moment of alignment could sour the mood and mark you as the perpetual contrarian in the room.

Ask whether the decision has been through the required risk and governance checks, relying on the process to surface the flaw rather than naming it yourself and owning the disruption that raising it would cause.

Name the flaw clearly even though it breaks the comfortable consensus, since a moment of easy agreement is exactly when a serious unspoken problem is most likely to slip through unchallenged and cause real harm.

Ask for the decision to be revisited at the next meeting once you've had time to test your concern, deferring rather than naming the flaw now while the group is aligned and the issue is actually on the table.

questions.question: You're overseeing a disciplinary involving an employee you personally find abrasive and have clashed with before. The evidence is genuinely borderline, and you notice yourself leaning toward the harsher reading.

options.text: Proceed but lean toward leniency to compensate, since bending the other way corrects for your dislike and a softer outcome avoids any appearance that personal history influenced your handling of the case.

Acknowledge your bias openly, hand the decision to someone impartial or rigorously check your reasoning against the evidence, since the integrity of the outcome matters more than your confidence in your own fairness.

Gather substantially more evidence to make the borderline case clearer before deciding, deferring the judgement rather than confronting the fact that your own bias is shaping how you're reading what you already have.

Apply the disciplinary matrix and sanctions guidance precisely to the facts, treating strict mechanical adherence to the framework as removing your personal feelings from the decision despite the borderline call.

questions.question: What does genuine accountability look like in a senior HR leader when something under their remit goes wrong?

options.text: Handling it in a way that protects the team's morale and reputation, since shielding people from fallout and keeping spirits up is what a supportive leader does when something has gone wrong on their watch.

Naming their own part plainly, without hedging or spreading blame, and being seen to do so — accountability that arrives wrapped in caveats and shared responsibility teaches everyone below them to do exactly the same.

Ensuring a thorough post-incident review is documented and the corrective actions are logged, since a properly recorded process and clear remediation steps are what demonstrate the issue was taken seriously.

Carefully establishing all the contributing factors before accepting any personal responsibility, since accountability should rest on a complete understanding of what happened rather than a premature admission.

questions.question: Leadership repeatedly leans on you to handle things that aren't HR's role and increasingly cross ethical lines — quiet exits, off-book deals — because you're capable and they trust you to be discreet.

options.text: Raise the pattern of inappropriate requests with the CHRO to set policy on it, framing it as an organisational governance question rather than personally telling leadership you won't do what they're asking.

Keep handling what you can do well while gently declining the worst asks, since being the trusted, capable person leadership turns to is a source of influence you'd lose by refusing them outright and too often.

Draw the line clearly, tell leadership which of these you won't do and why, and hold it even though being the dependable fixer has made you valued — because discretion used to cross ethical lines is just complicity.

Take each request as it comes and find reasons to slow-walk the ones you're uneasy about, avoiding a clear boundary conversation while quietly hoping the pattern of asks eases off over time on its own.

questions.question: A charismatic, dominant CEO has built momentum behind a people decision you believe is genuinely damaging. Everyone defers to them, and pushing back means being the one who challenges the founder in full flow.

options.text: Take your concerns to the board or other executives to build a coalition first, routing your challenge around the CEO rather than putting the disagreement to them honestly and directly to their face yourself.

Make the case against it directly and respectfully to the CEO, with the evidence and the human cost laid out plainly, since the value of your role is precisely being the person willing to challenge them when others won't.

Support the CEO's direction while looking for small ways to soften the impact, since openly opposing a charismatic founder rarely changes their mind and mostly costs you the access you need to influence them later.

Ask for time to model the consequences thoroughly before the decision is finalised, deferring your challenge into analysis rather than directly telling the CEO you believe the decision they're set on is damaging.

questions.question: You can hit a visible target this quarter — and the recognition that comes with it — by pushing through a people decision you know will create real problems a year from now, long after the credit has been banked.

options.text: Commission a fuller impact assessment before committing either way, deferring the choice rather than openly acknowledging that you'd be trading a known future harm for a short-term result and the credit it brings.

Proceed since the decision meets this quarter's targets and the approved criteria, treating satisfaction of the current objectives as the legitimate basis for the call without weighing the longer-term harm you foresee.

Decline the short-term win that stores up real harm, and make the case openly for the slower right course even though it forfeits this quarter's recognition, since trading the future for a metric is a poor bargain.

Take the win this quarter and manage the consequences when they arrive, since delivering visible results keeps stakeholders confident in you and a strong reputation now gives you more room to fix things later on.

questions.question: A live situation — a credible safeguarding concern about a manager — demands action now, but you don't have complete information and getting it would take days during which people remain at potential risk.

options.text: Handle it quietly and informally for now to avoid alarming anyone or damaging the manager prematurely, since a discreet approach keeps the situation contained while you work out what's really going on here.

Act proportionately now to protect people on the credible information you have, separating or suspending as needed, and accept the discomfort of deciding under uncertainty rather than leaving anyone at risk while you wait.

Refer it immediately to senior leadership and Legal to decide what to do, since a safeguarding concern about a manager is too serious and high-risk for the HRBP to take protective action on without sign-off.

Hold off on any action until you've gathered complete information, since taking a serious step against a manager on incomplete facts could be unfair and you want to be certain before doing anything irreversible.

questions.question: A change programme you championed and staked your credibility on clearly isn't working. Persisting would protect your reputation for a while; admitting it and reversing course would be a visible climbdown.

options.text: Continue delivering against the approved programme plan and milestones, treating adherence to the mandated plan as the correct course until it is formally reviewed, regardless of what the results are already showing.

Call it openly — say the approach isn't working, take responsibility, and change course — since protecting your reputation by persisting with something you know is failing costs the organisation far more than the climbdown.

Commission a thorough review of the programme before deciding whether to change course, deferring the admission until an analysis makes the case for reversal undeniable rather than owning the failure now.

Keep the programme going while quietly adjusting it, since an outright reversal would unsettle the people who backed it on your word and a more gradual pivot protects both their confidence and your standing.

questions.question: Why does emotional composure under hostility matter so much for a senior HR leader, beyond simply staying professional?

options.text: Because staying steady is what lets you keep telling the truth when someone powerful is trying to rattle you out of it — composure isn't about looking calm, it's about not letting intimidation change the position you hold.

Because remaining measured ensures the leader keeps following due process and the agreed protocols even under pressure, so that hostility never causes a deviation from the correct and consistent procedure.

Because a calm, warm presence reassures people and de-escalates conflict, helping preserve relationships and goodwill even in tense moments so that everyone leaves the encounter feeling heard and respected.

Because staying detached lets the leader avoid being drawn into a charged exchange and buys time to consider matters fully, so no response is given in the heat of the moment that might later prove to be unwise.

questions.question: In a meeting, a powerful leader is publicly humiliating a junior employee — belittling and demeaning them in front of peers. Everyone looks away. Intervening means confronting the aggressor in the moment.

options.text: Step in then and there to stop it and protect the person's dignity, and address the behaviour with the leader directly afterwards, since looking away while someone is demeaned in front of others makes you complicit.

Wait until after the meeting to assess what happened and gather the employee's account before acting, since intervening publicly on the strength of one heated exchange could be an overreaction you can't take back.

Smooth the moment over to defuse the tension and check on the employee privately later, since openly confronting a powerful leader mid-meeting would escalate things and embarrass everyone, including the junior.

Raise the leader's conduct formally with their manager and HR afterwards, routing it through the proper channel rather than challenging the humiliation in the moment when it is actually happening to the employee.

questions.question: Anxious stakeholders want firm assurances about an uncertain outcome you can't actually guarantee. Giving them the confident promise they want now would calm them and make you look in control.

options.text: Avoid committing either way by emphasising how many variables are still in play, deflecting the request for assurance into a discussion of complexity rather than being straight about what you genuinely can't promise.

Give the reassurance they're looking for to settle their anxiety, since confidence is steadying in uncertain times and you can manage expectations down later if the outcome turns out differently than hoped.

Be honest about what you can and can't promise, set out what you're doing and the real uncertainties, since a confident promise you can't keep buys calm today at the cost of your credibility when it unravels.

Point them to the agreed plan and governance milestones as the basis for confidence, treating reference to the formal process as a substitute for an honest account of the real uncertainty in the outcome.

questions.question: You discover a small error in a settlement calculation that favours the company — the departing employee is owed slightly more. They've already signed, won't ever know, and no one is checking.

options.text: Correct it and make sure the employee gets what they're actually owed, since doing the right thing only when someone is watching isn't integrity — and how you act when no one would know is the truest measure of it.

Note the discrepancy and hold off acting unless it's ever raised, since reopening a closed settlement has complications and you'd want to be sure of the obligation before disturbing an agreement that's already done.

Leave it, since the matter is closed, the employee has signed and moved on, and reopening a settled agreement over a small sum would create awkwardness for everyone with no real benefit to anyone involved.

Treat the signed agreement as final per the standard settlement process, since the employee accepted the terms and the documentation is complete, which closes the matter regardless of the calculation error found.

questions.question: What does genuine influence for an HR leader ultimately rest on, as distinct from positional authority or persuasion tactics?

options.text: The ability to marshal compelling evidence for every position, since influence rests on arguments that are so thoroughly substantiated that they cannot reasonably be resisted once they are properly laid out.

Deep mastery of the frameworks, data and processes, since an HR leader who is unquestionably expert and consistent commands the technical authority that makes the business defer to their recommendations.

Strong, trusting relationships built across the organisation, since people are most readily influenced by someone they like and feel genuinely connected to, and rapport is what opens the door to changing minds.

A track record of being right and honest even when it was costly — people are influenced over time by someone they've seen tell hard truths and hold up under pressure, not by charm or the authority of a title.

questions.question: A talented member of your team is clearly capable of one day taking your role. You could quietly limit their exposure to protect your position, or actively develop and champion them at some risk to yourself.

options.text: Support them warmly while keeping the highest-profile opportunities for yourself, since maintaining your own standing lets you keep mentoring them from a secure position rather than risking being eclipsed too soon.

Manage their development strictly through the formal talent and succession process, treating adherence to the standard pathway as the right approach without personally championing them beyond what it requires.

Actively develop and champion them, putting them forward for the exposure they've earned, since a leader who stunts talent to protect their own position fails the very job they're trying to hold on to.

Develop them gradually while waiting to see how committed and ready they really are, holding back the biggest opportunities until you're fully certain of their readiness rather than backing them wholeheartedly now.

questions.question: An organisational decision conflicts sharply with a value you hold and have spoken about. Staying silent now would be the politically safe choice; objecting openly would mark you out and cost you support.

options.text: Raise the values concern through a formal channel like an ethics committee, routing your objection into a process rather than personally and openly taking the stand that would actually mark you out on it.

Object openly and explain why the decision conflicts with the principle you've stood for, accepting the political cost, since a value you only uphold when it's safe was never really a value you held at all.

Keep your objection private and work within the system to soften the decision, since openly breaking with the organisation would burn the relationships and standing you need to influence things constructively later.

Hold off and gather more context on the decision before reacting, giving yourself room to reconsider whether it really conflicts with the principle rather than committing to a costly public stance prematurely.

questions.question: A difficult issue lands on your desk that you could resolve yourself with some hard conversations, or pass up to your director — who would take it on, but at the cost of looking like you couldn't handle it.

options.text: Handle it yourself through the hard conversations it needs, escalating only what genuinely requires your director's authority, since reaching for escalation to dodge a difficult conversation is avoidance in disguise.

Pass it to your director framed as keeping them informed, since they'd manage it smoothly and a well-judged escalation that keeps a senior leader in the loop reflects good stakeholder awareness rather than weakness.

Sit on it while you assess whether it's really yours to own or better escalated, taking time to weigh the options rather than committing to the difficult conversations it would take to deal with it directly now.

Escalate it per the issue-management protocol that routes matters of this type upward, treating adherence to the documented escalation path as the correct action regardless of whether you could resolve it yourself.

questions.question: A long-running, thorny people problem in another team keeps getting handed around and quietly dropped because it's hard and politically awkward. It's now drifted to you, and dropping it again would be easy and unremarkable.

options.text: Take it on properly and see it through despite the difficulty and the political awkwardness, since a problem everyone keeps abandoning is exactly the one that does real damage when no one is willing to own it.

Smooth it over enough to keep the affected people comfortable for now, since a problem this politically sensitive is better managed lightly than forced to a head in a way that strains relationships across teams.

Spend time fully scoping the problem and its history before committing to act, since something this entrenched needs to be thoroughly understood first, and a hasty intervention could make a delicate situation worse.

Log it and route it to whichever function formally owns issues of this kind, treating correct allocation through the process as discharging your part rather than taking on a problem that keeps being passed around.

questions.question: Everyone in the room has talked themselves into a decision you believe is genuinely wrong — not just suboptimal, but a betrayal of how the organisation should treat people. You're the only one unconvinced, and the meeting is moving to close it out.

options.text: Ask for the decision to be deferred pending more analysis, using the request for more work to slow it down rather than stating outright that you believe the decision is wrong and refusing to endorse it now.

Say plainly, before it's closed, that you can't support it and exactly why, putting your dissent on the record and standing behind it — because if no one is willing to be the lone voice, wrong decisions pass unchallenged.

Push for the decision to be taken to a higher forum before it's finalised, routing your objection upward rather than owning your dissent openly in the room where the decision is actually being made right now.

Let it pass to preserve the unity of the team and raise your concerns quietly with individuals afterwards, since being the sole holdout in a closing meeting marks you out and rarely changes the outcome anyway.

questions.question: A vendor's AI psychometric tool claims 91% predictive validity and a leader wants to adopt it for executive hiring immediately. The claim is impressive but unverified and the methodology isn't disclosed.

options.text: Tell the leader plainly you won't put executive hiring on an unverified vendor claim, insist on seeing the independent validation and bias evidence first, and accept slowing the rollout over the leader's enthusiasm.

Check the vendor holds the relevant certifications and meets the procurement checklist and approve it if it does, treating satisfaction of the formal vendor-onboarding criteria as confirming the tool is sound to use.

Move ahead with a pilot to keep the leader's momentum, since the validity figure is strong, the vendor is credible, and being the enabler who helps the business adopt promising tools builds your standing with them.

Suggest a lengthy evaluation programme studying the tool across many cycles before any use, deferring adoption indefinitely into analysis rather than giving the leader a clear position on whether to trust the claim.

questions.question: A vendor's HR chatbot reportedly resolves 85% of Tier 0 and Tier 1 queries with no human. A leader wants to deploy it and cut the service desk. What most needs to be true before you'd support it?

options.text: Confirm the chatbot meets the data-protection and procurement requirements and approve it on that basis, treating compliance with the onboarding checklist as sufficient without examining how it handles real cases.

Propose an extended multi-phase pilot studying every query type before committing, deferring any decision on the chatbot or the headcount into prolonged evaluation rather than taking a clear position on it now.

Support the deployment and the headcount saving the leader wants, since an 85% resolution rate is a strong result and being the partner who enables an efficient, modern service keeps you well-regarded by them.

Insist on knowing what happens to the other 15% and the sensitive cases, and that a clear human route exists for them, telling the leader that headline deflection rates hide the queries people most need a human for.

questions.question: An AI screening tool you use is found to reject candidates from one ethnic group at a markedly higher rate. A leader wants to keep using it because it's efficient and 'the algorithm is just being objective'.

options.text: Refer the disparity to Legal and IT to rule on whether the tool can continue, since algorithmic discrimination is a complex compliance question that should be owned above the HRBP rather than acted on by you.

Commission a deeper statistical study to confirm the disparity is genuinely caused by the tool before acting, since an adverse-impact finding needs to be fully substantiated before you stop using a relied-on system.

Stop using it pending a fix, telling the leader plainly that an algorithm producing adverse impact isn't 'objective' but discriminating at scale, and that efficiency is no defence for a tool that screens out a protected group.

Keep using it while raising the concern softly, since it's efficient, the leader values it, and challenging a tool the business relies on too forcefully would put you at odds with a leader who's clearly sold on it.

questions.question: Leadership is excited about AI that scores employee productivity and 'engagement' by monitoring keystrokes, messages and activity. They see it as smart management; you see intrusive surveillance with thin validity.

options.text: Tell leadership plainly that pervasive monitoring corrodes trust, rests on shaky validity, and likely breaches privacy law, and push back on deploying surveillance dressed up as analytics — even against their enthusiasm.

Check the monitoring can be covered by updating the acceptable-use and privacy policies and proceed if it can, treating the existence of a policy basis as resolving whether the surveillance should be done at all.

Help shape a more palatable version of the monitoring that gives leadership the insight they want, since they're set on it and finding an acceptable way to deliver what they're asking keeps you a valued partner.

Suggest a long evaluation of the tool's accuracy and legal position before any rollout, deferring the question into study rather than directly telling leadership that the surveillance approach is itself the wrong path.

questions.question: What is the most important distinction between using AI as an automation tool versus as a decision-support tool in HR?

options.text: Decision-support tools require closer governance and documentation than automation tools, so the key is ensuring each type is onboarded through the correct procurement and compliance process for its category.

Automation tends to be more disruptive to adopt than decision-support, so HR should favour decision-support tools that augment managers gently and are easier to introduce without unsettling people or ways of working.

The distinction matters mainly for risk assessment, so the priority is analysing each tool thoroughly to classify it correctly before deployment, ensuring nothing is adopted until its category is firmly established.

Automation replaces human execution of routine tasks; decision-support informs a judgement a human still owns — and HR must insist decisions affecting people's livelihoods never quietly slide from the second into the first.

questions.question: Your AI tools technically keep a 'human in the loop', but in practice managers approve the AI's recommendations in seconds without scrutiny. A leader is comfortable with this — it's fast and the AI is 'usually right'.

options.text: Confirm the process meets the documented 'human review' requirement and treat that as sufficient, since a human formally approves each output the governance standard for human oversight is technically satisfied.

Tell the leader a rubber-stamp isn't real human oversight, and push to make the human check meaningful — slower if needed — since 'usually right' still means the tool is wrong about real people no one is catching.

Suggest studying how often the quick approvals diverge from a careful review before changing anything, deferring action into analysis rather than telling the leader the oversight has effectively become meaningless.

Leave the fast-approval flow as it is since it works and the leader likes the speed, and look for light ways to encourage a bit more scrutiny without slowing a process everyone is happy with as it currently stands.

questions.question: An AI tool now contributes to performance ratings, but its logic can't be explained. Employees rated low are asking why, and you can't actually tell them. A leader says the model's track record speaks for itself.

options.text: Suggest reviewing the model's documentation to see whether explanations can be derived before responding to employees, deferring their questions while you investigate rather than confronting the opacity problem now.

Tell the leader that a rating you can't explain to the person it affects isn't defensible or fair, and push to either make it explainable or remove it from decisions about people — accepting that this limits a tool they like.

Help craft general explanations employees will accept without exposing the model's opacity, since the tool performs well and giving people a reassuring rationale keeps the process running smoothly for the leader.

Point employees to the appeals process in the performance policy and let that handle objections, treating the existence of a formal route to challenge as sufficient without addressing why the rating can't be explained.

questions.question: To save time, a manager wants to use generative AI to write disciplinary and termination letters and send them with little or no human review. The drafts look polished but sometimes state things inaccurately.

options.text: Suggest auditing a sample of past AI-drafted letters for accuracy before deciding on a review policy, deferring the safeguard into study rather than insisting now that these letters always get a proper human check.
