Let the manager use the AI drafts with a quick glance to keep things efficient, since the output is mostly good, they're busy, and supporting a time-saving way of working keeps you helpful in their eyes.

Insist every such letter is properly reviewed and owned by a human before it goes out, telling the manager that an AI-drafted inaccuracy in a termination letter is the company's liability and a real person's livelihood.

Confirm the AI drafting tool is on the approved-software list and permit its use on that basis, treating the tool's formal approval as sufficient without requiring meaningful human review of each letter's content.

questions.question: Why does data quality matter so much when an HR team adopts an AI tool, beyond it being a technical concern?

options.text: Because clean data makes the tool's results more convincing to stakeholders, helping HR win confidence and adoption for the system across the business so its benefits can be realised without resistance.

Because poor data undermines confidence in the outputs, so the priority is exhaustively cleansing and validating every dataset before the tool is used, ensuring no decision rests on data that isn't fully verified.

Because data-quality standards must be documented and met as part of the tool's onboarding, since adhering to the defined data-governance requirements is what makes the deployment compliant and properly controlled.

Because a model trained on the organisation's flawed or biased history will faithfully reproduce those flaws at scale — and HR has to be willing to challenge a confident tool whose outputs simply launder the past.

questions.question: Your team has come to rely almost entirely on one vendor's AI platform for core HR decisions. A leader is happy — it's seamless — but you can see the over-reliance creating a serious single point of failure.

options.text: Raise the over-reliance plainly and push to retain human capability and alternatives even though it adds cost and friction, since handing core people decisions wholesale to one opaque system is a risk no one is owning.

Leave the seamless setup as it is since the leader values it and it works well, and avoid raising a risk that would mean adding cost and complexity to a system everyone is currently happy to depend on.

Refer the concentration risk to IT and procurement to manage, since vendor dependency and resilience are technology-governance matters that sit above the HRBP to assess and address on their own initiative.

Commission a full dependency and risk assessment before raising anything, since flagging over-reliance on a working system needs to be backed by a thorough analysis of the actual exposure before you act on it.

questions.question: A leader wants to feed employee records into an external AI vendor's platform to generate insights, without a proper data-processing agreement or telling employees their data is being used this way.

options.text: Check whether the existing privacy notice could be read to cover the use and proceed if it arguably can, treating a technical policy basis as sufficient without genuinely informing employees or securing a proper agreement.

Suggest a lengthy legal and data-protection review before sharing anything, deferring the matter into process rather than telling the leader plainly that doing this without consent and an agreement is not acceptable.

Refuse to share the data without a proper agreement and transparency, telling the leader that quietly handing employee records to a third-party AI is unlawful and a betrayal of trust, whatever insight it promises.

Find a way to get the insights the leader wants while keeping the data-sharing low-profile, since the analytics would be valuable and being the partner who unlocks them keeps you well-positioned with the leader.

questions.question: On the strength of a vendor's promises, a leader wants to cut a chunk of the HR team now, before the AI that's meant to replace their work has been proven to actually do it reliably.

options.text: Tell the leader plainly it's reckless to cut the people before the tool has proven it can do the work, and push to validate the AI in real conditions first — even though it delays a saving the leader wants right now.

Support the leader's plan and manage the headcount reduction smoothly, since they're confident in the technology and being the partner who enables the cost saving they've decided on keeps you valued by them.

Refer the headcount-versus-AI decision to the CHRO and Finance to own, since betting staffing levels on an unproven tool is a strategic risk that should be decided above the HRBP rather than acted on by you.

Propose an extended evaluation of the AI before any decision on headcount, deferring both indefinitely into study rather than telling the leader directly that cutting the team now, on a promise, is the wrong call.

questions.question: An AI tool flags an employee as a high 'flight risk'. Their manager, rather than supporting them, quietly starts excluding them from key projects and succession plans to 'reduce the disruption when they go'.

options.text: Suggest reviewing the accuracy of the flight-risk model before intervening, since challenging a manager's response to it needs confidence the prediction is reliable before you raise concerns about how they're using it.

Confirm the manager is using the flight-risk output within the tool's intended parameters and leave it there, treating use of the model as designed as appropriate without questioning how the manager is acting on it.

Challenge the manager directly, name that acting on a prediction to sideline someone becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy and is unfair, and insist the flag prompt a supportive conversation, not a quiet demotion.

Help the manager plan around the predicted departure discreetly, since the tool flagged a real risk and supporting the manager's contingency planning is a sensible, responsive use of the insight the AI provided.

questions.question: Why does the explainability of an automated or AI-assisted decision matter for HR, beyond regulatory compliance?

options.text: Because employees accept decisions more readily when they're given a clear rationale, so explainability helps maintain trust and smooth acceptance of outcomes that might otherwise generate complaints or resistance.

Because a decision you can't explain to the person affected can't be defended as fair, challenged or corrected — and HR's duty is to refuse to let opaque outputs make calls about people that no one can actually account for.

Because unexplained decisions are harder to defend if challenged, so the safe approach is to analyse and document the basis of each one thoroughly before it's issued, ensuring nothing goes out that can't be justified.

Because explainability requirements are set out in data-protection law and must be satisfied, so the priority is ensuring each automated decision meets the documented standard for providing the required explanation.

questions.question: A planned bias audit of a new AI hiring tool is inconvenient and behind schedule. A leader wants to skip it and go live, arguing the vendor has 'already tested it for fairness' and the launch date matters.

options.text: Go live on schedule trusting the vendor's testing as the leader suggests, since the vendor is reputable, the date matters to the business, and being the partner who keeps the project on track keeps you well-regarded.

Propose deferring the whole launch until a comprehensive independent audit can be scheduled and completed, pushing the date back indefinitely rather than getting your own focused check done in time for go-live.

Insist on your own bias audit before go-live, telling the leader that relying on the vendor's word about their own product is no substitute for testing it on your people and roles — and that the launch date can wait.

Obtain the vendor's fairness-testing certification for the file and proceed, treating possession of the vendor's documented assurance as satisfying the fairness requirement without conducting your own audit at all.

questions.question: A leader wants to present AI-generated workforce insights to the board as hard, objective findings. You know the model's assumptions and limitations make several of the conclusions far shakier than they'll look.

options.text: Suggest commissioning further validation of the model before presenting anything to the board, deferring the presentation into more analysis rather than being straight that the conclusions are being overstated now.

Ensure the insights are presented in the standard analytics reporting format the board expects, treating conformity with the established reporting template as sufficient without flagging how uncertain the outputs are.

Insist the limitations and assumptions be shown alongside the findings, telling the leader that presenting shaky model outputs to the board as objective fact will mislead a decision and rebound on you both later.

Present the insights cleanly and confidently as the leader wants, since caveats would weaken the story for the board, and a compelling, uncluttered picture is what lands well and reflects well on the function.

questions.question: You discover several HR staff are pasting sensitive employee information into public consumer AI tools to speed up their work. A leader shrugs it off — 'everyone does it now' — and doesn't want to make an issue of it.

options.text: Suggest assessing the scale of the practice and the data exposed before doing anything, deferring action into a review rather than moving now to stop sensitive employee data being pasted into public AI tools.

Tell the leader plainly this is a serious data breach waiting to happen, and put clear guidance and a safe alternative in place now, rather than let 'everyone does it' normalise leaking employee data into public tools.

Circulate a reminder of the acceptable-use policy and consider the matter addressed, treating issuance of the policy reminder as discharging the obligation without changing the behaviour or providing a safe tool.

Take the relaxed line the leader prefers and avoid making a fuss, since the staff are just trying to be efficient, clamping down would be unpopular, and the leader clearly doesn't want this turned into a problem.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason certain people decisions should never be fully delegated to an AI system, however capable it becomes?

options.text: Because decisions that materially affect someone's livelihood carry moral and legal accountability a model can't hold — and HR has to insist a human remains genuinely answerable, not just nominally in the loop.

Because the technology isn't yet proven enough to be fully trusted, so the prudent course is to keep humans involved until far more evidence accumulates that the systems can be relied on without supervision.

Because employees are more comfortable knowing a person is involved, so keeping a human visible in the process protects trust and acceptance even where the AI could technically handle the decision on its own.

Because regulations and policy require human involvement in significant automated decisions, so the priority is ensuring each such decision is structured to meet the documented human-oversight requirement.

questions.question: A leader wants to run AI sentiment analysis on employees' internal messages and emails to identify who is 'disengaged' and target them. The communications were never intended to be analysed in this way.

options.text: Refuse to support covert analysis of private communications, telling the leader it's intrusive, legally risky and destroys trust, and that disengagement is addressed by talking to people, not by surveilling their messages.

Check whether updating the monitoring policy could provide a basis and proceed if it can, treating the existence of a policy hook as resolving whether scanning employees' communications for sentiment is acceptable.

Help design a softer version focused on aggregate sentiment to give the leader the insight they want, since they're keen on it and finding a more palatable way to deliver it keeps you a responsive partner to them.

Propose a thorough legal and ethical review of the approach before any analysis, deferring the matter into process rather than telling the leader directly that analysing private messages this way is the wrong path.

questions.question: To win approval and budget for an HR AI project you believe in, you're tempted to present the vendor's most optimistic ROI and adoption figures to the board, downplaying the real risks and uncertainties.

options.text: Lead with the optimistic figures to get the project over the line, since you believe in it, a compelling business case is how things get funded, and you can manage expectations down once it's underway and delivering.

Present an honest case with the realistic range and the genuine risks, even though it's less dazzling, since winning approval on inflated numbers sets the project up to be judged a failure and burns your credibility.

Hold off seeking approval until you've gathered enough data to model every scenario precisely, deferring the proposal indefinitely rather than putting forward an honest, decision-ready case with the uncertainty acknowledged.

Present the figures exactly as the vendor supplied them in the standard business-case format, treating faithful reproduction of the vendor's numbers as sufficient without owning a realistic assessment of the risks.

questions.question: A leader is excited about an AI interview tool that scores candidates by analysing facial expressions and tone of voice. The science behind such 'emotion analysis' is widely contested and likely discriminatory.

options.text: Check the vendor's compliance documentation and validation claims and proceed if they're in order, treating the vendor's own assurances as sufficient without questioning whether emotion-analysis hiring is sound at all.

Suggest an extensive evaluation of the tool's accuracy and fairness before any use, deferring the decision into prolonged study rather than telling the leader the approach itself rests on contested pseudoscience.

Trial it cautiously alongside normal interviews to satisfy the leader's interest, since they're enthusiastic, the demo was impressive, and being open to the tools the business is curious about keeps you well-regarded.

Tell the leader plainly the science is contested and the tool risks discriminating against neurodivergent and disabled candidates, and refuse to put hiring decisions on pseudoscience however impressive the demo looks.

questions.question: When an AI tool produces a recommendation that turns out to be wrong and harms an employee, where should accountability sit?

options.text: Primarily with the vendor who built the tool, so the organisation can point to the supplier's responsibility, preserve internal trust, and avoid the fallout landing on the managers who relied on the recommendation.

With the humans and the organisation that chose to deploy and act on it — and HR must insist on that ownership upfront, rather than let 'the system recommended it' become a way for everyone to dodge responsibility.

It can only be settled after a full investigation establishes exactly how the error arose, so the priority is a thorough analysis before assigning responsibility to any party for what the tool recommended at all.

With whoever failed to follow the documented review process, since accountability is correctly assigned by tracing which step in the approved procedure was not carried out as the governance framework requires.

questions.question: A leader wants to roll out AI tools that influence pay, progression and scheduling without telling employees they're being used, reasoning that disclosure would only generate anxiety and pushback.

options.text: Keep the rollout quiet as the leader prefers and handle questions if they arise, since proactive disclosure would stir anxiety, the tools are helpful, and avoiding unnecessary alarm keeps the introduction smooth.

Confirm the privacy notice technically references automated processing and proceed, treating the existence of a buried policy mention as satisfying transparency without genuinely informing employees of the tools' use.

Suggest a communications-readiness study before deciding what to disclose, deferring transparency into analysis rather than telling the leader plainly that employees must be informed about tools that affect them.

Insist employees be told how AI is being used in decisions that affect them, telling the leader that hiding it is both likely unlawful and a guaranteed trust disaster when it surfaces — which it always eventually does.

questions.question: Facing redundancies, a leader wants to let an AI model select who goes based on a scoring algorithm, treating its ranked output as the decision so the process feels 'objective and impartial'.

options.text: Propose validating the model's scoring against past cases before using it, deferring into analysis rather than confronting the deeper problem of letting an algorithm decide who loses their job in the first place.

Use the AI ranking as the basis while adding a light human sign-off, since it gives the leader the objective-looking process they want and supporting an efficient, defensible-seeming approach keeps you well-positioned.

Refuse to let the model's output be the decision, telling the leader redundancy selection demands human judgement and accountability, and that an algorithm scoring people for exit is a legal and moral hazard, not impartiality.

Confirm the model's selection criteria are documented and consistently applied and proceed on that basis, treating a consistent, recorded algorithm as making the selection fair regardless of who actually owns it.

questions.question: A vendor's promotion-recommendation AI was trained on your industry's historical data — an era when senior roles went overwhelmingly to one demographic. A leader wants to trust its picks as 'data-driven'.

options.text: Use the recommendations as helpful input the leader values while applying your own judgement on top, since the tool surfaces useful signals and being open to it keeps you aligned with a leader sold on the approach.

Confirm the tool meets the procurement and data-governance checklist and rely on its output, treating completion of the onboarding requirements as sufficient without examining the bias baked into its training data.

Tell the leader plainly that a model trained on a biased past will recommend a biased future and call that 'data-driven', and refuse to let it drive promotions until it's been corrected and tested for adverse impact.

Commission a detailed study of the model's historical training data before raising concerns, deferring into analysis rather than telling the leader now that trusting its picks would replicate past discrimination.

questions.question: What does meaningful informed consent for using employees' data in an AI system genuinely require?

options.text: That the consent wording is reviewed exhaustively by legal and data-protection experts before anything proceeds, ensuring the language is watertight before any employee data is ever used in the system at all.

That people genuinely understand what's done and could realistically decline without penalty — and HR must be honest that consent buried in onboarding paperwork or coerced by the power imbalance isn't real consent at all.

That a signed consent statement meeting the documented requirements is obtained and filed for each employee, since holding a properly completed consent record is what evidences compliance with the legal standard.

That employees are given a clear, reassuring explanation that makes them comfortable with the use, so the organisation secures broad agreement and can proceed with the AI initiative without generating resistance.

questions.question: Leadership wants to deploy a major HR AI system at speed to beat competitors, explicitly skipping the testing and governance steps you'd normally insist on. The pressure to move now is intense.

options.text: Find ways to compress the timeline and get it live fast as leadership wants, since speed matters competitively and being the partner who makes ambitious things happen quickly keeps you valued in the room.

Refer the decision to skip governance to the CHRO and risk committee, since launching a major AI system without the usual controls is a risk that should be owned above the HRBP rather than decided by you.

Propose a full governance and testing programme on the normal timeline regardless of the pressure, effectively deferring the launch without directly engaging leadership's demand to move at speed now to compete.

Hold the line on the essential testing and governance, telling leadership that being first with a tool that mishandles people's data or decisions is a far worse outcome than being second — and accept being the brake.

questions.question: A leader wants to roll out an AI mental-health chatbot as the front line of employee wellbeing support, partly to reduce demand on human services. You're worried about how it handles someone in genuine crisis.

options.text: Insist on knowing exactly how it escalates a crisis to a human and that it never becomes a barrier to real help, telling the leader that fronting wellbeing with a bot to cut costs is dangerous if someone vulnerable falls through.

Confirm the chatbot meets the vendor's safety certification and the procurement criteria and approve it, treating the documented assurances as sufficient without testing how it actually responds to someone in crisis.

Propose an extended evaluation of the chatbot's crisis handling before any rollout, deferring the launch into prolonged study rather than setting clear, non-negotiable safeguards for escalation now and proceeding.

Support the rollout to deliver the efficiency the leader wants, since the chatbot handles routine support well and being the partner who enables a modern, scalable wellbeing offer keeps you well-regarded by them.

questions.question: A manager wants to use generative AI to write employment references at scale. You've seen the drafts invent specifics — projects and achievements that didn't happen — though they read convincingly.

options.text: Stop the practice, telling the manager that issuing references containing invented facts is dishonest and exposes the company to liability, and that every reference must be accurate and verified by a human before it goes.

Let the manager keep using the AI with a light edit to remove obvious errors, since it saves real time, the drafts are mostly fine, and supporting an efficient way of working keeps you helpful in their eyes.

Suggest auditing a batch of AI-drafted references for accuracy before setting any policy, deferring the safeguard into review rather than insisting now that references can't go out containing fabricated specifics.

Confirm the AI tool is on the approved-software register and permit its use, treating the tool's formal approval as sufficient without requiring that each reference be checked for invented or inaccurate content.

questions.question: What is HR's distinctive responsibility in governing AI tools that affect people, as opposed to leaving it to IT or Legal?

options.text: Being the function that insists on the human and ethical questions others may miss — fairness, dignity, accountability, the impact on real people — and being willing to say no to a technically compliant tool that treats people wrongly.

Acting as the bridge that helps the business adopt AI smoothly, since HR's value is bringing people along and ensuring new tools are welcomed rather than resisted across the workforce as they are introduced.

Ensuring every AI tool affecting employees passes the required HR policy and compliance checks, since HR's role is to apply the relevant people-related governance criteria consistently to each system before use.

Providing thorough analysis of the people-related risks of each tool, so that no AI system affecting employees is adopted until HR has fully assessed and documented its potential impact on the workforce first.

questions.question: An AI ranks applicants and auto-rejects below a cut-off. You spot a candidate with exactly the unconventional background the role needs, filtered out because their CV doesn't match the patterns the model learned.

options.text: Suggest reviewing how often the model rejects strong non-standard candidates before intervening, deferring action into analysis rather than bringing this clearly suitable candidate back into the process right now.

Respect the model's cut-off since the candidate scored below the defined threshold, treating consistent application of the algorithm's scoring as the fair and correct basis for the decision regardless of the role's needs.

Override the model to bring the candidate back into consideration, and flag that a tool auto-rejecting the unconventional profiles you actually need is a flaw — even though overriding it undercuts the system's selling point.

Leave the model's decision in place to keep the process consistent and the system credible, since overriding it for one candidate would undermine confidence in the tool the business has invested in and adopted.

questions.question: Evidence is mounting that an expensive AI system you helped procure isn't delivering and is making poor recommendations. Leadership, having championed it publicly, wants the disappointing data kept quiet.

options.text: Surface the evidence honestly and push to fix or withdraw the tool, telling leadership that hiding that it's making poor calls about people to protect an investment compounds the harm and the eventual reckoning.

Call for an extended review to confirm the system is genuinely underperforming before raising it, deferring into analysis rather than telling leadership now that it's making poor recommendations about real people.

Help manage the narrative and give the system more time, since leadership backed it publicly, the data may improve, and protecting their position while quietly working on it keeps you aligned with the people you serve.

Continue operating the system per the approved deployment plan while logging the issues, treating adherence to the mandated rollout as the correct course until a formal review concludes, despite the live harm.

questions.question: A predictive model labels certain employees 'likely leavers'. A leader wants to quietly stop investing development and opportunities in them to avoid 'wasting it on people who'll go anyway'.

options.text: Suggest validating the attrition model's accuracy before acting on its labels, deferring into analysis rather than telling the leader directly that using it to deny people development is both unfair and counterproductive.

Challenge it head-on, naming that withholding opportunity from people a model tagged makes the prediction come true and is unfair and self-defeating, and insist the flag never be used to quietly write people off.

Confirm the model is being used within its documented purpose and leave the leader's approach in place, treating use of the tool as designed as appropriate without questioning the decision to withhold opportunity.

Help the leader direct investment toward the employees the model rates as stayers, since focusing limited development budget where it's most likely to pay off is a reasonable, data-informed call you can support.

questions.question: A large automated process is about to push through decisions affecting thousands of employees' pay in one run. Everyone is satisfied it's been tested; you alone feel a human sample-check before release is essential.

options.text: Ask for yet more testing documentation before the release rather than a live check, deferring into paperwork rather than insisting on the practical human verification you actually believe the run needs right now.

Let it proceed since it's been tested and the team is confident, and avoid being the one person holding up a process everyone else is ready to release, which would only mark you out as overly cautious here.

Insist on a human sample-check before the run goes out and hold that even when everyone else is ready, since an automated error at this scale lands on thousands of real people and someone has to be willing to slow it.

Confirm the process passed every step of the validation protocol and approve the release on that basis, treating successful completion of the documented testing as sufficient without the human check you feel is needed.

questions.question: Leadership wants you to put your name and HR's endorsement behind launching an AI system you have serious, unresolved doubts about — its fairness, its accountability, its effect on people. The pressure to sign off is heavy.

options.text: Pass the sign-off decision up to the CHRO or an AI governance board, since endorsing a system this significant is a call that should be owned above the HRBP rather than rest on your personal endorsement at all.

Give the endorsement leadership is asking for while noting your reservations privately, since they're set on launching, your sign-off keeps you aligned with them, and you can keep working to improve the tool from inside.

Decline to endorse it until your doubts are genuinely resolved, set out plainly what would have to change, and accept the pressure and disappointment rather than lend your name to a system you can't actually stand behind.

Ask for more time and more analysis before signing anything, deferring the endorsement into open-ended study rather than telling leadership clearly that you won't stand behind the system as it currently stands.
